**Expected Keil Observation:** P1.0 alternates between 0 V and 5 V every 20 ms → 25 Hz square wave (period 40 ms) visible in the Logic Analyser.

---

**(b)** *Explain how the **Keil µVision Simulator** allows observation of port pin states and timer overflow without physical hardware. List the steps to view P1 in the simulator. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

The **Keil µVision Simulator** is a software emulation environment that models the 8051 CPU, SFRs, port latches, and timer/counter hardware bit-by-bit. It uses the loaded `.hex` file to execute instructions cycle-accurate, and provides the following observation tools:

1. **Peripheral Menu → I/O-Ports → Port 1** — Shows a checkbox grid representing the 8 latched bits of P1. Each tick / click in the program updates the visible logic state in real time.
2. **View → Logic Analyser Window** — Records the digital waveform of selected pins (P1.0, P3.4, etc.) over time. The student measures period, frequency, and duty cycle directly.
3. **View → Watch Window** — Add SFRs like `TMOD`, `TCON`, `TH0`, `TL0`, `TF0` to monitor their hexadecimal value during single-stepping.
4. **Step Into (F11) / Step Over (F10)** — Executes instructions one at a time, allowing observation of how `TH0:TL0` increments and how the TF0 flag is set.
5. **Reset and Run (F5)** — Executes the full program; student can pause at any time to capture the port state.

**Steps to View P1 in the Simulator:**

1. Build the project (F7) and enter **Debug mode** (Ctrl+F5 or click the *Debug* magnifying glass).
2. Open **Peripherals → I/O-Ports → Port 1** from the menu bar.
3. Click *Run* (F5) or *Step* (F11) — the checkboxes for P1.0–P1.7 toggle in real time as the program modifies the SFR.
4. Open **Analysis Windows → Logic Analyser** → *Setup* → add P1.0 → *Run* → observe the square-wave trace.
5. Use *Stop* and measure the *Time Axis* to read off the period in microseconds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> Marks are deducted if a student writes *"connect LED to P1.0 and observe on CRO"* — in a **simulation** exam you **MUST** mention *Keil Logic Analyser* or *Ports window*. Hardware CRO/multi-meter references are valid only for the **hardware lab cycle**, not simulation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** *With the help of a neat C program, explain how Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** is used to generate a baud rate of **9600** for serial communication at 11.0592 MHz. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Baud-rate formula for 8051 UART:**

$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{osc}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

With **SMOD = 0** (default), $f_{osc} = 11.0592\ \text{MHz}$, and target baud = 9600:

$$
9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11\,059\,200}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11\,059\,200}{32 \times 12 \times 9600} = \frac{11\,059\,200}{3\,686\,400} = 3
$$

Therefore:

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

**C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>
void serial_init(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x20;     // Timer 1, Mode 2 (auto-reload)
    TH1  = 0xFD;     // Reload value for 9600 baud
    SCON = 0x50;     // Mode 1, 8-bit UART, REN enabled
    PCON &= 0x7F;    // Clear SMOD (bit 7) → SMOD = 0
    TR1  = 1;        // Start Timer 1
}
void main(void)
{
    serial_init();
    while(1) {
        SBUF = 'A';           // Load character
        while(TI == 0);       // Wait until transmitted
        TI = 0;               // Clear TI flag
    }
}
```

**Simulated verification:** In Keil's *Serial Window* (View → Serial Window #1), the user will see the character 'A' transmitted at exactly 9600 baud — confirming the timer reload value.

---

**(b)** *Write an 8051 C program to count the number of external pulses applied to pin **P3.4 (T0)** and display the count (in binary) on **Port P2** using Counter Mode. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** When the C/T̄ bit in TMOD is set to **1**, Timer 0 acts as a **counter** of external pulses arriving at pin P3.4. Every HIGH-to-LOW transition increments TL0. On overflow from FFH → 00H, the TF0 flag is set.

**C Program:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>
void main(void)
{
    TMOD = 0x06;     // Timer 0, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
                     // C/T_bar = 1 → Counter mode
                     // TH0 = 0x00 (auto-reload value)
    TH0  = 0x00;
    TL0  = 0x00;
    TR0  = 1;        // Start counter
    while(1) {
        P2 = TL0;    // Display count on P2 (simulated)
    }
}
```

**Explanation of key instructions:**

- `TMOD = 0x06` → Binary `0000 0110` → Timer 0 in **Mode 2**, **Counter** mode, software-controlled, internal gate.
- The counter increments on every negative edge of the signal at P3.4.
- After every 256 pulses, the counter **auto-reloads** from `TH0` (00H), so it counts cyclically 0–255.
- `P2 = TL0` continuously reflects the count — visible in the **Keil Ports Window** as toggling checkboxes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. Forgetting to set the **C/T̄ bit** (using timer mode by accident) — pulses on P3.4 will be ignored.
> 2. Trying to display count > 255 without using a software variable to track overflows.
> 3. Not clearing the TF0 flag inside a polling loop (causes missed counts).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **TMOD = 0x01** → Timer 0, Mode 1, software start, timer mode (most common exam default).
- [x] **TMOD = 0x02** → Timer 0, Mode 2, 8-bit auto-reload (best for baud-rate & repetitive delays).
- [x] **TMOD = 0x06** → Timer 0, **Mode 2 + Counter mode** (counts external pulses on P3.4).
- [x] **TCON address = 88H**, bit-addressable; **TR0 = bit 4** (start), **TF0 = bit 5** (overflow flag).
- [x] **f_osc = 11.0592 MHz** is KTU-standard because it makes baud-rate math exact (9600 ⇒ TH1 = 0xFD).
- [x] For 16-bit Mode 1: $T_{delay} = (65536 - \text{count}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$.
- [x] For 8-bit Mode 2: $T_{delay} = (256 - THx) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$.
- [x] Maximum delay in Mode 1 (no software loop) = 71.1 ms (at 11.0592 MHz).
- [x] For longer delays, use a **nested software counter** multiplying the timer overflow.
- [x] Always **reload THx:TLx inside the ISR** (Mode 1) — Mode 2 reloads automatically.
- [x] **Keil Simulation View:** `Peripherals → I/O-Ports → Port 1` (logic level) and `Analysis → Logic Analyser` (waveform).
- [x] **Watch Window** is used to observe SFRs like `TMOD`, `TCON`, `TH0`, `TL0`, `TF0`.
- [x] **Interrupt vector** for Timer 0 = `000BH`; for Timer 1 = `001BH`.
- [x] The Keil simulator uses **cycle-accurate emulation** — register values exactly match real hardware behaviour.
- [x] In *Counter Mode*, max pulse frequency = $f_{osc} \div 24$ (≈ 460 kHz for 11.0592 MHz).
- [x] **GATE = 1** in TMOD enables hardware control (INT0/INT1 pin) of timer start/stop — rarely used in basic lab.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Serial Communication
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL507 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Examples of Serial Communication

# Examples of Serial Communication — KTU 2024 Scheme Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Module Experiment 2 (PCICL507)** focuses on the practical implementation of three major serial communication protocols on a standard 8-bit microcontroller (typically the **AT89C51/52** family or compatible). Mastery of these examples is mandatory for the Continuous Evaluation (CE) record, the Lab Practical End-Semester Examination, and the viva voce.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Serial Communication** is a data transmission methodology in which information bits are propagated sequentially over a single communication channel (or a single differential pair), one bit per clock cycle, as opposed to transmitting an entire word simultaneously across multiple parallel wires.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCICL507)** syllabus, three canonical examples are mandated:

1. **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver–Transmitter)** — Asynchronous, full-duplex, 2-wire protocol.
2. **SPI (Serial Peripheral Interface)** — Synchronous, full-duplex, 4-wire master–slave protocol.
3. **I²C (Inter-Integrated Circuit)** — Synchronous, half-duplex, 2-wire multi-master protocol.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Terminology** — Always refer to the protocols by their full academic names during viva. Examiners frequently award grace marks for precise vocabulary: *asynchronous*, *synchronous*, *full-duplex*, *half-duplex*, *master–slave*, and *bit-banging*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "One-Lane Road vs. Multi-Lane Highway"

Imagine two cities need to exchange a truckload of 8 boxes:

- **Parallel Communication** is a **8-lane highway**: all 8 boxes travel simultaneously and arrive together at the destination. It is fast, but building 8 lanes is expensive, and in crowded cities it wastes road space.
- **Serial Communication** is a **single-lane road**: the 8 boxes leave one after another, in a strict order. It is slower, but only one lane is needed — cheaper, simpler, and perfect for long distances.

To make the single lane work, both cities must agree on:
- The **speed of the trucks** (Baud Rate).
- The **starting signal** (Start bit / Chip Select / Start Condition).
- The **ending signal** (Stop bit / deselect / Stop Condition).
- The **traffic rules** (clock polarity, phase, acknowledge bits, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Constants to Memorize**
> - **TTL Logic Level High (V_IH)** = **2.0 V minimum**
> - **TTL Logic Level Low (V_IL)** = **0.8 V maximum**
> - **RS-232 Logic High (SPACE/Logic 0)** = **+3 V to +15 V**
> - **RS-232 Logic Low (MARK/Logic 1)** = **−3 V to −15 V**
> - **Standard Baud Rates**: 1200, 2400, 4800, **9600** (most common), 19200, 38400, 57600, 115200 bps.
> - **Standard I²C Address**: **7-bit** (theoretical max 127 slaves per bus).

### 1.3 The Three KTU-Mandated Examples — At a Glance

| # | Example Name | Synchronous? | Wires | Duplex | Typical Use in KTU Lab |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **UART — PC ↔ 8051** | No (Async) | 2 (TX, RX) | Full | Sending alphanumeric strings to HyperTerminal/Tera Term |
| 2 | **SPI — 8051 ↔ 7-Segment / ADC** | Yes | 4 (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS) | Full | Driving MAX7219 display or MCP3008 ADC |
| 3 | **I²C — 8051 ↔ AT24C04 EEPROM / DS1307 RTC** | Yes | 2 (SDA, SCL) | Half | Reading/Writing serial EEPROM or Real-Time Clock |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Example 1 — UART Serial Communication (Asynchronous)

**Operational Logic (Bullet Steps):**

- The 8051's **on-chip UART (SBUF register, SCON, PCON)** handles framing automatically.
- Data is framed as: **1 Start bit (LOW) → 8 Data bits (LSB first) → 1 Stop bit (HIGH)**. Optionally a 9th data bit and parity may be configured.
- **Baud Rate is generated by Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)**. The 8051 has no dedicated baud-rate generator, so Timer 1 overflow becomes the bit-tick.
- The **PC ↔ 8051** voltage mismatch is solved by the **MAX232** level shifter IC (RS-232 ↔ TTL).
- Data flow is **full-duplex** because TXD (P3.1) and RXD (P3.0) are independent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 11.0592 MHz Crystal?** — This is the *de facto* standard crystal for 8051 serial work. Its value is a multiple of **384** (32 × 12) and **192** (32 × 6), so TH1 reload values become **exact integers** for every standard baud rate. Using 12 MHz produces non-integer TH1 values that cause framing errors.

**Example 2 — SPI Serial Communication (Synchronous, 4-Wire)**

- Operated via **bit-banging** on the 8051 (since classic 8051 lacks a hardware SPI engine) using four general-purpose I/O pins.
- The **Master** drives the clock on **SCK**, and asserts **SS (Slave Select, active LOW)** to address a specific slave.
- Data shifts out of **MOSI** (Master Out, Slave In) and shifts in via **MISO** (Master In, Slave Out) — one bit per clock edge.
- **No addressing is needed on the bus**; chip-select lines serve the role of addressing.

**Example 3 — I²C Serial Communication (Synchronous, 2-Wire)**

- Only **two lines** are shared: **SDA (Serial Data)** and **SCL (Serial Clock)** — both are open-drain, requiring **pull-up resistors (typically 4.7 kΩ to +5 V)**.
- The **Start Condition (S)** is defined as **SDA falling while SCL is HIGH**; the **Stop Condition (P)** is **SDA rising while SCL is HIGH**.
- The first byte transmitted after START is always the **7-bit slave address + 1 R/W bit**. The addressed slave returns an **ACK (ACKnowledge)** by pulling SDA LOW during the 9th clock pulse.
- Classic KTU experiment: write the string *"KTU"* into an **AT24C04 EEPROM** and read it back.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units | Standard Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 8051 Baud Rate (Mode 1) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | $f_{\text{osc}}$ = crystal freq, TH1 = timer reload, SMOD = PCON.7 | bps | Timer 1 in Mode 2 |
| 2 | TH1 (SMOD = 0) | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \cdot \text{Baud}}$ | — | Decimal count | Default power-on |
| 3 | TH1 (SMOD = 1) | $\text{TH1} = 256 - \dfrac{f_{\text{osc}}}{192 \cdot \text{Baud}}$ | — | Decimal count | Double baud rate |
| 4 | SCON Configuration | $\text{SCON} = 0x50$ | Mode 1, REN enabled | Hex | 8-bit UART, RX enabled |
| 5 | PCON Doubler | $\text{PCON} = 0x80$ | Sets SMOD = 1 | Hex | Doubles baud rate |
| 6 | SPI Throughput | $\text{Throughput} = f_{\text{SCK}}$ | $f_{\text{SCK}}$ is master clock | bps | One bit per clock |
| 7 | I²C Pull-up Resistor | $R_{\text{pull}} \approx 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$ | Pull-up to V_DD | Ohms | Standard-mode 100 kHz |
| 8 | I²C Bus Capacitance Limit | $C_{\text{bus}} \leq 400 \text{ pF}$ | Total wire + pin capacitance | Farads | Limits max nodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Escape rule for the table above** — every absolute-value or "such that" symbol was rendered with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to preserve the markdown pipe boundary. The same rule applies to your own KTU answer scripts: use $\vert v \vert$ for magnitude in any inline math.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **UART** is the backbone of GPS modules, Bluetooth HC-05/HC-06, GSM SIM800, and debug consoles in every embedded product from a washing machine to a satellite.
- **SPI** dominates high-speed peripherals — SD cards, TFT displays, ADCs, DACs, and flash memory — wherever bandwidth beats pin-count.
- **I²C** is the lingua franca of low-speed sensor boards — temperature sensors (LM75), RTCs (DS1307), EEPROMs (AT24C series), and GPIO expanders (PCF8574) all live on the same two wires.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — TH1 Value for 9600 Baud, 11.0592 MHz Crystal, SMOD = 0

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal** — Derive the exact Timer 1 reload value that the 8051 must use to communicate at 9600 bps with a 11.0592 MHz crystal — the most common KTU lab configuration.

**Step 1 — Identify the master equation.**

The 8051 UART baud rate generator (Mode 1, Timer 1 in Mode 2) obeys the canonical relation:

$$
\text{Baud Rate} \;=\; \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

where the factor **12** is the number of oscillator periods per machine cycle, the factor **32** is the UART's oversampling rate, and **$2^{\text{SMOD}}$** is 1 (SMOD = 0) or 2 (SMOD = 1).

**Step 2 — Substitute known quantities.**

- $f_{\text{osc}} = 11.0592 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz}$
- Baud Rate = **9600 bps**
- SMOD = 0  $\Rightarrow$  $2^{\text{SMOD}} = 1$

$$
9600 \;=\; \frac{1}{32} \cdot \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}
$$

**Step 3 — Isolate the denominator.**

Multiply both sides by $32 \cdot 12$:

$$
9600 \cdot 384 \;=\; \frac{11.0592 \times 10^{6}}{256 - \text{TH1}}
$$

$$
3\,686\,400 \;=\; \frac{11\,059\,200}{256 - \text{TH1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $(256 - \text{TH1})$.**

Invert and multiply:

$$
256 - \text{TH1} \;=\; \frac{11\,059\,200}{3\,686\,400} \;=\; 3
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for TH1.**

$$
\text{TH1} \;=\; 256 - 3 \;=\; 253
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to hexadecimal (C51 syntax).**

$$
253_{(10)} \;=\; 0xFD_{(16)}
$$

**Step 7 — Final register value (typical KTU initialization block).**

$$
\boxed{\text{TH1} = 0xFD, \quad \text{SCON} = 0x50, \quad \text{PCON} = 0x00, \quad \text{TMOD} = 0x20}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is integer-exact** — Because $11.0592 \times 10^6$ is a multiple of $384 \times 9600$, TH1 lands precisely on 253 with zero fractional remainder. This is the single most-examined derivation in the **PCICL507** viva voce.

### 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Three Examples Side-by-Side

| Parameter | Example 1 — UART (PC ↔ 8051) | Example 2 — SPI (8051 ↔ MAX7219) | Example 3 — I²C (8051 ↔ AT24C04) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous?** | No (async) | Yes | Yes |
| **Wires used** | 2 (TX, RX) + GND | 4 (SCK, MOSI, MISO, SS) | 2 (SDA, SCL) + GND |
| **Speed @ 11.0592 MHz** | 9600 bps typical | Up to ~1 Mbps (bit-bang) | 100 kHz standard / 400 kHz fast |
| **Master–Slave?** | Peer-to-peer (no master) | Strict 1 master, N slaves | Multi-master capable |
| **Addressing** | None (point-to-point) | Hardware chip-select line | 7-bit address in first byte |
| **Hardware on 8051** | On-chip SBUF, SCON | None — bit-bang | None — bit-bang |
| **Pull-up Resistors** | Not needed | Not needed | **Mandatory (4.7 kΩ)** |
| **KTU Output Device** | Tera Term on PC | 7-Segment / LCD via MAX7219 | EEPROM byte echoed on PC |

### 3.3 Worked Walk-Through — A Complete "Hello KTU" UART Transmission

Consider the canonical C51 program listed below. The byte sequence sent on the wire is calculated as follows for the character **'K'** (ASCII 0x4B = 0100 1011 binary):

**On-wire frame for 'K' (LSB-first, 10 bits total):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0 (Start)} &= 0 \\
\text{Bit 1 (D0)} &= 1 \quad (LSB) \\
\text{Bit 2 (D1)} &= 1 \\
\text{Bit 3 (D2)} &= 0 \\
\text{Bit 4 (D3)} &= 1 \\
\text{Bit 5 (D4)} &= 0 \\
\text{Bit 6 (D5)} &= 0 \\
\text{Bit 7 (D6)} &= 0 \\
\text{Bit 8 (D7)} &= 1 \quad (MSB) \\
\text{Bit 9 (Stop)} &= 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Total frame on TXD pin: **0 1 1 0 1 0 0 0 1 1** — exactly 10 bit-periods at 9600 bps = **1.0417 ms per character**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Explanation** — Step Bit 1 begins with the LSB because the 8051 UART transmits least-significant-bit first, a frequently-confused detail in viva questions. Step Bit 9 is the Stop bit, which is *always* a logic HIGH in standard UART; if the receiver sees a LOW here, it reports a **framing error**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete UART Lab Setup (PC ↔ 8051)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pcterm[PC with Tera Term] -- RS232 TXD --> max232in[MAX232 Level Shifter]
    pcterm2[PC with Tera Term] -- RS232 RXD --> max232in
    max232in -- TTL TXD --> mcuat[AT89C51 Microcontroller RXD P3 0]
    max232in -- TTL RXD --> mcuat2[AT89C51 Microcontroller TXD P3 1]
    mcuat -- 5V DC --> vreg[7805 Voltage Regulator]
    mcuat -- 11.0592 MHz --> xtal[Crystal Oscillator]
    mcuat -- 10uF plus 30pF --> resetcap[Power On Reset RC Network]
    mcuat -- EA pin HIGH --> ea[Execute from internal ROM]
    vreg -- 12V DC input --> dcpower[DC Adapter]
    xtal -- two 30pF --> gndA[Ground]
```

**Functional Interpretation of the Block Diagram:**

- The **PC's Tera Term** issues characters via the **RS-232 COM port**.
- The **MAX232** IC performs the bidirectional voltage translation between RS-232 (±12 V) and TTL (0–5 V) levels using its on-chip charge-pump capacitors.
- The **AT89C51** processes each byte through its hardware UART (SBUF, SCON, PCON) and either displays it, echoes it, or stores it.
- The **7805 regulator** drops the 12 V adapter output to a stable 5 V rail.
- The **11.0592 MHz crystal** is the heartbeat that defines the baud rate.

### 4.2 Timing Diagram — SPI Single-Byte Transfer (Master Reads 0xA5 from Slave)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startnode[SPI Transaction Begins] --> sslow[Master pulls SS LOW for slave]
    sslow --> sckrise[SCK rises 8 times for 8 bits]
    sckrise --> bit1[Bit 0 shifted out MOSI and in MISO]
    bit1 --> bit2[Bit 1 shifted out MOSI and in MISO]
    bit2 --> bit3[Bit 2 shifted out MOSI and in MISO]
    bit3 --> bit4[Bit 3 shifted out MOSI and in MISO]
    bit4 --> bit5[Bit 4 shifted out MOSI and in MISO]
    bit5 --> bit6[Bit 5 shifted out MOSI and in MISO]
    bit6 --> bit7[Bit 6 shifted out MOSI and in MISO]
    bit7 --> bit8[Bit 7 shifted out MOSI and in MISO]
    bit8 --> sshigh[Master pulls SS HIGH to end frame]
    sshigh --> done[SPI Transaction Complete]
```

**Functional Interpretation:**

- **SS goes LOW** to "wake up" the chosen slave and ignore all others on the bus.
- **8 clock pulses** on SCK transfer exactly 8 bits in each direction simultaneously (MOSI and MISO).
- **SS goes HIGH** to "release" the slave; during this HIGH time the bus is considered idle.

### 4.3 I²C Bus Topology with Two Slaves

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    masterNode[8051 Master Microcontroller] -- SDA line --> sdaBus[I2C Serial Data Line]
    masterNode -- SCL line --> sclBus[I2C Serial Clock Line]
    sdaBus -- 4 7k pullup --> vdd5[Plus 5V Rail]
    sclBus -- 4 7k pullup --> vdd5
    sdaBus --> eepromNode[AT24C04 EEPROM Address 0x50]
    sdaBus --> rtcNode[DS1307 RTC Address 0x68]
    sclBus --> eepromNode
    sclBus --> rtcNode
```

**Functional Interpretation:**

- The two pull-up resistors are **non-negotiable** for I²C; the bus lines are open-drain, so without pull-ups the lines float.
- Each slave listens to the bus and checks whether the first 7 bits after the START condition match its hard-wired address.
- The **DS1307 address is 0x68** and the **AT24C04 base address is 0x50** — these appear in almost every KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between UART, SPI, and I²C serial communication protocols in terms of synchronization, number of wires, and typical data rate.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **UART** is **asynchronous** (no clock line), uses **2 wires (TX, RX)**, and typically operates at **9600–115200 bps** in 8051-based KTU labs. *(1 mark)*
- **SPI** is **synchronous** (SCK line present), uses **4 wires (MOSI, MISO, SCK, SS)**, and supports **high speeds up to several Mbps**. *(1 mark)*
- **I²C** is **synchronous**, uses only **2 wires (SDA, SCL)**, supports **multi-master**, and operates at **100 kHz (standard) or 400 kHz (fast)**. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Model]`
**Why is a crystal frequency of 11.0592 MHz preferred over 12 MHz for 8051 serial communication? Justify with the TH1 calculation for 9600 baud.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- The 8051's baud-rate formula is $\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$. *(1 mark)*
- For 11.0592 MHz, $\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD$ — an **exact integer**, eliminating baud-rate error. *(1 mark)*
- For 12 MHz, the same formula gives $\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{12 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} = 256 - 3.255 = 252.745$ — a **fractional value** that introduces framing errors at the receiver. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> For Part B, the most common reasons students lose 2–3 marks are:
> 1. **Forgetting to clear the RI/TI flag** by software (`TI = 0;` after transmission).
> 2. **Configuring the wrong SCON mode** — Mode 1 is 8-bit UART, NOT Mode 2 (which is 9-bit).
> 3. **Mixing up TXD and RXD** — TXD is P3.1, RXD is P3.0 on the 8051.
> 4. **Omitting the MAX232 level shifter** in circuit diagrams when connecting to a PC's RS-232 port.
> 5. **Missing pull-up resistors** in I²C circuit diagrams.
> 6. **Failing to state SMOD value explicitly** when calculating TH1.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — UART Experiment

> `[KTU University Exam – Model, Module 2, Part B Choice A]`

**(a)** Design an 8051-based system to transmit the string **"KTU2024"** continuously to a PC at **9600 bps** using an 11.0592 MHz crystal. Draw the circuit diagram, write the full Embedded C program, and calculate the TH1 value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram (2 marks):** (Refer to Block Diagram in Section 4.1)

- 8051 with crystal 11.0592 MHz, 30 pF capacitors to GND, 10 kΩ reset pull-up, 10 µF electrolytic on RESET.
- MAX232 IC with four 1 µF charge-pump capacitors: T1IN ↔ 8051 TXD (P3.1), R1OUT ↔ 8051 RXD (P3.0).
- DB9 connector wired as standard RS-232 DTE (TXD pin 2, RXD pin 3, GND pin 5).
- Power supply 5 V via 7805 from 12 V adapter.

**Step 2 — TH1 Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\text{TH1} = 256 - \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600} = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

**Step 3 — Embedded C Program (3 marks):**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void uart_init(void) {
    SCON  = 0x50;     // Mode 1, 8-bit UART, REN enabled
    TMOD  = 0x20;     // Timer 1 in Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH1   = 0xFD;     // Reload value for 9600 bps
    TR1   = 1;        // Start Timer 1
}

void uart_tx(char ch) {
    SBUF = ch;        // Load byte into serial buffer
    while (TI == 0);  // Wait until transmission complete
    TI = 0;           // Clear TI flag manually
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned char *s = "KTU2024\r\n";
    unsigned char i;
    uart_init();
    while (1) {
        i = 0;
        while (s[i] != '\0') {
            uart_tx(s[i]);
            i++;
        }
    }
}
```

**(b)** Explain the **MAX232** level shifter with its internal charge-pump circuit. Why is it necessary to interface the 8051 with a PC's RS-232 port? Mention the standard voltage levels. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Voltage-level mismatch (2 marks):** The 8051 uses **TTL levels** — Logic 1 = +5 V, Logic 0 = 0 V. The PC's RS-232 port uses inverted logic with **±12 V** levels — Logic 0 (called SPACE) = +3 V to +15 V, Logic 1 (called MARK) = −3 V to −15 V.
- **MAX232 internal architecture (3 marks):** The MAX232 contains a **dual charge-pump** voltage doubler/inverter. With four external 1 µF capacitors, it generates **+10 V and −10 V** rails from a single +5 V supply. It has **two drivers (T1IN/T1OUT, T2IN/T2OUT)** and **two receivers (R1IN/R1OUT, R2IN/R2OUT)**.
- **Connection (1 mark):** T1IN connects to 8051 TXD, R1OUT connects to 8051 RXD, T1OUT connects to PC RXD (DB9 pin 2), R1IN connects to PC TXD (DB9 pin 3), and GNDs of both sides are tied together.
- **Why it is mandatory (1 mark):** Direct connection of ±12 V RS-232 signals to 8051's 5 V I/O pins will **destroy the microcontroller** immediately; MAX232 provides safe, bidirectional level translation.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — I²C Experiment

> `[KTU University Exam – Model, Module 2, Part B Choice B]`

**(a)** Interface an **AT24C04 serial EEPROM** to the 8051 using the I²C protocol (bit-banged on P1.0 = SCL and P1.1 = SDA). Write a full Embedded C program to **write the byte 0x55 to memory address 0x0020** and then **read it back and transmit it to the PC via UART at 9600 bps**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — SCL and SDA definitions (1 mark):**

```c
sbit SCL = P1^0;
sbit SDA = P1^1;
```

**Step 2 — I²C primitive functions (4 marks):**

```c
void i2c_start(void) {
    SDA = 1; SCL = 1;       // Bus idle
    i2c_delay();
    SDA = 0; i2c_delay();   // START: SDA falls while SCL is HIGH
    SCL = 0;
}

void i2c_stop(void) {
    SDA = 0; SCL = 0; i2c_delay();
    SCL = 1; i2c_delay();
    SDA = 1; i2c_delay();   // STOP: SDA rises while SCL is HIGH
}

void i2c_write(unsigned char d) {
    unsigned char i;
    for (i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
        SDA = (d & 0x80) ? 1 : 0;
        SCL = 1; i2c_delay();
        SCL = 0; i2c_delay();
        d <<= 1;
    }
    SDA = 1;                // Release SDA for ACK
    SCL = 1; i2c_delay();   // Slave pulls SDA LOW to ACK
    SCL = 0;
}

unsigned char i2c_read(void) {
    unsigned char i, d = 0;
    for (i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
        SCL = 1; i2c_delay();
        d = (d << 1) | SDA;
        SCL = 0; i2c_delay();
    }
    return d;
}
```

**Step 3 — Write and Read routines (1 mark):** Standard pattern: START → slave address (0xA0 for write, 0xA1 for read) → word address → data byte → STOP.

**Step 4 — UART initialization (1 mark):** SCON = 0x50, TMOD = 0x20, TH1 = 0xFD, TR1 = 1.

**(b)** Explain the **START and STOP conditions** of the I²C bus, the role of the **ACK bit**, and why **pull-up resistors** are mandatory on SDA and SCL. Show the SDA/SCL timing waveform. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **START condition (S) (1.5 marks):** A HIGH-to-LOW transition on the SDA line **while SCL is HIGH**. All I²C transactions begin with this signal; it is generated by the master and alerts all slaves to listen for their address.
- **STOP condition (P) (1.5 marks):** A LOW-to-HIGH transition on the SDA line **while SCL is HIGH**. It marks the end of a transaction and releases the bus.
- **ACK bit (2 marks):** After every byte, the receiver (master for writes, slave for reads) must pull SDA LOW during the 9th clock pulse. A missing ACK (SDA stays HIGH) is called a **NACK** and indicates either no slave present or a communication error.
- **Pull-up resistors (2 marks):** I²C drivers are **open-drain** — they can only pull the line LOW. When no device is driving, the line must be pulled HIGH by an external resistor. The standard value is **4.7 kΩ to +5 V**; the value depends on bus capacitance (limit **400 pF**) and the number of devices on the bus.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module Experiment 2 (PCICL507)**

- [ ] **Three protocols = Three experiments**: UART (PC link), SPI (display/ADC), I²C (EEPROM/RTC).
- [ ] **UART is asynchronous, full-duplex, 2-wire, no clock line**.
- [ ] **SPI is synchronous, full-duplex, 4-wire, master-driven clock on SCK**.
- [ ] **I²C is synchronous, half-duplex, 2-wire, open-drain with mandatory pull-ups**.
- [ ] **Baud-rate formula**: $\text{Baud} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}}}{32} \cdot \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$.
- [ ] **TH1 (SMOD=0)** = $256 - \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{384 \times \text{Baud}}$.
- [ ] **TH1 (SMOD=1)** = $256 - \frac{f_{\text{osc}}}{192 \times \text{Baud}}$.
- [ ] **9600 bps with 11.0592 MHz crystal** → TH1 = 0xFD = 253.
- [ ] **Always clear the TI flag** in software after `SBUF = ch;` transmission.
- [ ] **Standard SFR initializations**: SCON = 0x50, TMOD = 0x20, PCON = 0x00 (SMOD=0) or 0x80 (SMOD=1).
- [ ] **MAX232** is mandatory between 8051 (TTL) and PC (RS-232 ±12 V).
- [ ] **I²C START**: SDA falls while SCL is HIGH; **STOP**: SDA rises while SCL is HIGH.
- [ ] **I²C addressing**: 7-bit slave address in the first byte, MSB first, followed by 1 R/W bit.
- [ ] **I²C pull-up resistors** = **4.7 kΩ** to V_DD, mandatory for both SDA and SCL.
- [ ] **SPI Chip Select (SS)** is active LOW; pulling it HIGH releases the slave from the bus.
- [ ] **8051 UART pins**: TXD = P3.1, RXD = P3.0.
- [ ] **8051 I²C bit-bang pins (typical)**: SDA = P1.1, SCL = P1.0.
- [ ] **8051 SPI bit-bang pins (typical)**: SCK = P1.4, MOSI = P1.5, MISO = P1.6, SS = P1.7.
- [ ] **Data frame for UART Mode 1**: 1 Start + 8 Data (LSB first) + 1 Stop = 10 bits.
- [ ] **For 8051, there is no hardware SPI or I²C engine**; all KTU lab code uses **bit-banging**.
- [ ] **Common viva question**: "What happens if SMOD = 1?" → **Baud rate doubles**, TH1 formula changes.
- [ ] **Common viva question**: "Why open-drain on I²C?" → To allow **wired-AND arbitration** between multiple masters.
- [ ] **Always mention pull-up resistors and MAX232** in your circuit diagrams — examiners allocate dedicated marks for them.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing LED
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL507 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Interfacing LED

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LED Interfacing with a Microcontroller** is the fundamental embedded systems experiment in which a Light Emitting Diode (LED) is electrically and logically connected to a general-purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pin of a microcontroller (such as the 8051, ARM Cortex, or AVR family) such that the microcontroller can programmatically turn the LED **ON**, **OFF**, or **blink** it at a controlled rate. The interface requires a **current-limiting resistor (CLR)** placed in series with the LED to protect it from excessive forward current ($I_F$), which would otherwise cause thermal runaway and permanent device destruction.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCICL507 – Microcontroller Lab)**, Module Experiment 2 (Interfacing LED) is treated as the foundational **Output Device Interfacing** experiment. It establishes the baseline understanding required for all subsequent output-based experiments such as seven-segment displays, LCD modules, relays, buzzers, and stepper motors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Output Device Interfacing):**
> *Study of I/O port configurations, current sinking vs. current sourcing, electrical characteristics of GPIO pins, calculation of current-limiting resistor, and embedded C / Assembly programming for LED blinking, LED patterns, and LED sequencing.*

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water tap connected to a high-pressure water pipeline** (the microcontroller pin at **5V**). If you open the tap fully, water gushes out so violently that it destroys whatever container you place underneath. Now, place a **narrow pipe (current-limiting resistor)** between the tap and the container — the flow becomes gentle and controlled, perfect for filling the bucket (**LED**) without damaging it.

In this analogy:
- **Microcontroller pin (5V logic HIGH)** → High-pressure water pipeline
- **LED** → The bucket (light-emitting load)
- **Current-Limiting Resistor (CLR)** → Narrow control pipe
- **Microcontroller pin (0V logic LOW)** → Closed tap (no water = LED OFF)

When the microcontroller outputs a **logic HIGH (1)**, current flows from the pin → resistor → LED → ground, and the LED glows. When the microcontroller outputs a **logic LOW (0)**, there is no potential difference, and the LED remains OFF.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is an LED a "diode"?** Because it conducts current in **only one direction** (anode → cathode) and has a forward voltage drop ($V_F$) of approximately **1.8V–2.2V** for a standard red LED and **3.0V–3.6V** for blue/white LEDs. This unidirectional behavior is critical when determining which microcontroller logic level (HIGH or LOW) turns the LED ON.

---

## 1.3 Key Electrical Specifications (Standard Reference Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Red LED | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Voltage | $V_F$ | **1.8 – 2.2** | V |
| Forward Current | $I_F$ | **10 – 20** | mA |
| Reverse Breakdown Voltage | $V_R$ | **5.0** | V |
| Maximum Forward Current | $I_{F(max)}$ | **30** | mA |
| Viewing Angle | $\theta$ | **20° – 60°** | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> These values are **manufacturer-dependent**. Always consult the specific LED datasheet (e.g., Kingbright L-7113ID, Lite-On LTL-307) before computing the series resistor value for a lab experiment.

---

## 1.4 GPIO Pin Electrical Characteristics (8051 Reference)

For the KTU 2024 syllabus, the standard reference microcontroller is the **Intel 8051 family (e.g., AT89C51, P89V51RD2)**:

| Pin Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Logic HIGH Output Voltage | $V_{OH}$ | **2.4 (min), 4.5 (typ)** | V |
| Logic LOW Output Voltage | $V_{OL}$ | **0.4 (max), 0.1 (typ)** | V |
| Sourcing Current per pin (HIGH drive) | $I_{OH}$ | **−10 to −60** | μA |
| Sinking Current per pin (LOW drive) | $I_{OL}$ | **+1.6 to +10** | mA |
| $V_{CC}$ Supply Voltage | $V_{CC}$ | **+5.0** | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** The 8051 GPIO pin can **sink** much more current than it can **source**. Therefore, the most reliable LED interfacing topology is **Current Sinking** (LED connected between $V_{CC}$ and pin; pin LOW → LED ON). Standard red LEDs at 10–15 mA typically exceed the 8051's source capability, so **sink-mode is preferred in KTU lab examinations**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core "Why" Behind LED Interfacing

A microcontroller is fundamentally a **logic device** that operates at very low voltage and current levels. An LED, while simple, requires a **specific minimum forward current ($I_F \geq 5$ mA)** to become visibly luminous. Three fundamental questions must be answered before writing a single line of code:

1. **Electrical Question:** How much current will flow through the LED when the pin is HIGH, and is it within the safe operating range of both the LED AND the microcontroller pin?
2. **Topology Question:** Should we use **current sourcing** (pin drives current OUT) or **current sinking** (pin pulls current INTO ground)?
3. **Logical Question:** Which logic level (HIGH or LOW) will turn the LED ON, given the chosen topology?

---

## 2.2 Current-Sourcing vs. Current-Sinking Topology

### A. Current Sourcing (Active-HIGH Configuration)
- **Connection:** Microcontroller pin → Resistor → LED Anode → LED Cathode → GND
- **Operation:** Pin = **HIGH (1)** → LED ON; Pin = **LOW (0)** → LED OFF
- **Limitation:** 8051 source current is only ~60 μA, insufficient for bright LED illumination.

### B. Current Sinking (Active-LOW Configuration) ✅ *Preferred for 8051*
- **Connection:** $V_{CC}$ (5V) → Resistor → LED Anode → LED Cathode → Microcontroller pin
- **Operation:** Pin = **LOW (0)** → LED ON (current flows from $V_{CC}$ into the pin to ground); Pin = **HIGH (1)** → LED OFF
- **Advantage:** 8051 sink current up to 10 mA comfortably drives a standard LED.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU board examinations, the **sinking topology with Active-LOW logic** is the default expected answer unless otherwise specified in the question paper.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{I_F}$$ | Series resistor (sourcing mode, HIGH = ON) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 2 | $$R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F - V_{OL}}{I_F}$$ | Series resistor (sinking mode, $V_{OL}$ included) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 3 | $$I_F = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R}$$ | Verification: actual forward current | Amperes (A) |
| 4 | $$P_R = I_F^2 \cdot R$$ | Power dissipated by current-limiting resistor | Watts (W) |
| 5 | $$P_{LED} = V_F \cdot I_F$$ | Power dissipated by the LED | Watts (W) |
| 6 | $$T_{delay} = \frac{N \cdot 12}{f_{osc}}$$ | 8051 instruction cycle time (1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods) | Seconds (s) |
| 7 | $$N_{loop} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{machine}}$$ | Number of loop iterations for software delay | Dimensionless |
| 8 | $$Duty\% = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100$$ | PWM duty cycle for LED brightness control | Percent (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard resistor values follow the **E12 series**: 100, 120, 150, 180, 220, 270, 330, 390, 470, 560, 680, 820 $\Omega$ (and their decade multiples). The calculated value must be rounded **UP** to the next available standard value to ensure safe (non-excessive) LED current.

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of LED Interfacing

LED interfacing is **not merely a "hello world" experiment**. It forms the basis of:

- **Status indication** in industrial control panels (RUN, FAULT, READY lights)
- **Visual feedback** in consumer electronics (power-on indicators, charging status)
- **Optical data transmission** (IR LEDs in TV remotes, Li-Fi prototypes)
- **Traffic light controllers** (extension experiment in KTU curriculum)
- **Charlieplexing and Multiplexing** (driving N*(N-1) LEDs with N GPIO pins)
- **PWM-based dimming** (controlling LED brightness by varying duty cycle)

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU lab viva, when asked *"What is the real-world application of LED interfacing?"*, answer with **"Indicator panel design in embedded control systems, e.g., a washing machine status panel that shows Wash → Rinse → Spin → Done"** — this is a high-scoring application-oriented response.

---

## 2.5 Software Delay Generation in 8051

Since there is no built-in hardware timer in our basic LED program (Experiment 2, Module 2 typically uses software delay), we must generate a time delay using nested loops. The 8051 executes one **machine cycle** per 12 oscillator periods:

$$T_{machine} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

For the standard **11.0592 MHz crystal**, $T_{machine} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085\,\mu s$.

To produce a delay of 1 second, we need approximately $\frac{1}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 921,600$ machine cycles, which is typically achieved through a **double-nested loop** with FFH (255) as the inner counter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Current-Limiting Resistor Calculation

### Problem Setup
A standard red LED ($V_F = 2.0\,V$, $I_F = 15\,mA$) is to be interfaced to 8051 Port 1.0 using the **current-sinking topology**. Given $V_{CC} = 5.0\,V$ and the 8051's typical $V_{OL} = 0.4\,V$, determine the value of the current-limiting resistor and verify that the standard E12 value is safe.

### Step 1: Draw the Equivalent Circuit (KVL)

Applying **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the closed loop (sinking topology: $V_{CC}$ → R → LED → pin → GND):

$$V_{CC} = V_R + V_F + V_{OL}$$

> *Explanation:* The total supply voltage must equal the sum of the voltage drops across the resistor, the LED, and the LOW-level output voltage of the microcontroller pin.

### Step 2: Solve for the Resistor Voltage Drop

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{CC} &= V_R + V_F + V_{OL} \\
V_R &= V_{CC} - V_F - V_{OL} \\
V_R &= 5.0 - 2.0 - 0.4 \\
V_R &= 2.6\,V
\end{aligned}$$

> *Explanation:* Subtracting the LED's forward voltage drop and the pin's LOW-level output voltage from the supply gives the voltage that must be dropped across the current-limiting resistor.

### Step 3: Apply Ohm's Law to Find R

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= \frac{V_R}{I_F} \\
R &= \frac{2.6\,V}{15 \times 10^{-3}\,A} \\
R &= 173.33\,\Omega
\end{aligned}$$

> *Explanation:* Using Ohm's Law ($V = IR \Rightarrow R = V/I$), the calculated resistance is **173.33 Ω**.

### Step 4: Select the Nearest Standard E12 Resistor

From the E12 standard series, the next available value **above** 173.33 Ω is **180 Ω**.

> *Explanation:* We always round UP to the next standard value to ensure the actual current is **less than or equal to** the design $I_F$, providing a safety margin.

### Step 5: Verify the Actual Operating Current

$$\begin{aligned}
I_{F(actual)} &= \frac{V_{CC} - V_F - V_{OL}}{R_{selected}} \\
I_{F(actual)} &= \frac{5.0 - 2.0 - 0.4}{180} \\
I_{F(actual)} &= \frac{2.6}{180} \\
I_{F(actual)} &= 0.01444\,A \\
I_{F(actual)} &= 14.44\,mA
\end{aligned}$$

> *Explanation:* With the 180 Ω standard resistor, the actual LED current is **14.44 mA**, which is safely below the design target of 15 mA and well below the 30 mA maximum rating. ✅

### Step 6: Verify Resistor Power Rating

$$\begin{aligned}
P_R &= I_{F(actual)}^2 \cdot R_{selected} \\
P_R &= (0.01444)^2 \cdot 180 \\
P_R &= 2.085 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 180 \\
P_R &= 0.0375\,W
\end{aligned}$$

> *Explanation:* The resistor dissipates only **37.5 mW**. A standard **¼ watt (0.25 W) resistor** is more than sufficient (safety factor ≈ 6.67×). ✅

### Final Result Summary

| Parameter | Calculated Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Required Resistance | 173.33 | $\Omega$ |
| Selected E12 Resistor | **180** | $\Omega$ |
| Actual LED Current | **14.44** | mA |
| Resistor Power | 37.5 | mW |
| Resistor Wattage (use) | **¼ W (0.25 W)** | W |
| Logic Level to Turn ON | **LOW (0)** | — |

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Software Delay Calculation (1 Second)

### Problem Setup
Generate a **1-second delay** using an 8051 with an **11.0592 MHz crystal**, using a double-nested software delay loop.

### Step 1: Calculate the Machine Cycle Period

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{osc} &= \frac{1}{f_{osc}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \\
T_{osc} &= 90.42 \times 10^{-9}\,s = 0.09042\,\mu s \\
T_{machine} &= 12 \cdot T_{osc} \\
T_{machine} &= 12 \cdot 0.09042\,\mu s \\
T_{machine} &= 1.085\,\mu s
\end{aligned}$$

> *Explanation:* The 8051 requires 12 oscillator periods to complete one machine cycle (single-byte, single-cycle instruction such as `DJNZ`).

### Step 2: Calculate Required Machine Cycles for 1 Second

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{total} &= \frac{T_{desired}}{T_{machine}} \\
N_{total} &= \frac{1\,s}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}\,s} \\
N_{total} &= 921,600\,machine\,cycles
\end{aligned}$$

> *Explanation:* Approximately 921,600 instructions must be executed to consume 1 second of real time.

### Step 3: Determine Loop Counts for Double-Nested Loop

We use two registers: an inner counter (R1) and an outer counter (R0). Each iteration of the inner `DJNZ R1, label` loop consumes **2 machine cycles** (1 for `DJNZ`, 1 for the jump when R1 ≠ 0). Setting R1 = 255 (0FFH) gives **255 × 2 = 510 cycles** per outer iteration.

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{outer} &= \frac{N_{total}}{N_{inner}} \\
N_{outer} &= \frac{921,600}{510} \\
N_{outer} &= 1,807.06
\end{aligned}$$

> *Explanation:* Approximately 1,807 outer iterations are required. To make the program cleaner, we typically use R0 = F0H (240 decimal) and add a **third micro-delay** (e.g., a single `NOP` or a few extra instructions) to fine-tune. The KTU expected program for this is well-documented in standard 8051 textbooks.

### Step 4: Standard KTU 8051 Delay Subroutine (Assembly)

```assembly
DELAY:  MOV R1, #0FFH     ; 1 cycle
LOOP2:  MOV R0, #0FFH     ; 1 cycle
LOOP1:  DJNZ R0, LOOP1    ; 2 cycles × 255 = 510
        DJNZ R1, LOOP2    ; 2 cycles × 255 = 510
        RET
```

> *Explanation:* The inner `LOOP1` creates 510 machine cycles; the outer `LOOP2` repeats this 255 times, generating 510 × 255 = 130,050 cycles ≈ **141 ms**. For a true 1-second delay, the calling program invokes `DELAY` 8 times. This is the standard KTU 2024 expected approach.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Program Behavior Verification

| Program Step | Port 1 Pin State | LED Status (Sinking) | Register State |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Power-On | All pins HIGH (1) | All OFF | P1 = FFH |
| `CLR P1.0` | P1.0 = 0 (LOW) | LED0 ON | P1 = FEH |
| `SETB P1.0` | P1.0 = 1 (HIGH) | LED0 OFF | P1 = FFH |
| `MOV P1, #55H` | P1 = 01010101b | LEDs 0,2,4,6 ON | P1 = 55H |
| `MOV P1, #0F0H` | P1 = 11110000b | LEDs 4,5,6,7 ON | P1 = F0H |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power ON 8051] --> B[Initialize Port 1 as OUTPUT]
    B --> C[Set P1 to FFH All LEDs OFF]
    C --> D[Call DELAY Subroutine]
    D --> E[Clear P1.0 Logic LOW]
    E --> F[LED0 Turns ON Sinking Mode]
    F --> G[Call DELAY Subroutine]
    G --> H[Set P1.0 Logic HIGH]
    H --> I[LED0 Turns OFF]
    I --> D
    D -.Loop Forever.-> D
```

> *Explanation:* This is the **canonical infinite-loop flow** of a blinking LED program. The CPU never exits; it alternates between the ON-state delay and the OFF-state delay indefinitely. The "Loop Forever" dashed edge indicates the program counter wraps back to the first delay call.

---

## 4.2 LED Interfacing Hardware Connection Diagram (Mermaid Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[VCC 5V Supply] --> B[Resistor R 180 ohm]
    B --> C[LED Anode A]
    C --> D[LED Cathode K]
    D --> E[8051 Port Pin P1.0]
    E --> F[8051 Internal GND via Output Driver]
    G[Crystal 11.0592 MHz] --> H[8051 XTAL1 and XTAL2 Pins]
    I[EA Pin Tied HIGH] --> H
    J[Reset Circuit 10uF plus 10K] --> K[8051 RST Pin]
    H --> L[CPU executes MOV SETB CLR DJNZ instructions]
    L --> E
```

> *Explanation:* The block diagram traces the current path: $V_{CC}$ → R (180 Ω) → LED anode → LED cathode → Port pin P1.0 → internal ground driver. The auxiliary blocks (crystal, reset, $\overline{EA}$) are minimum connections required for the 8051 to execute the program. When P1.0 outputs logic LOW, the internal N-channel transistor of the output stage conducts, completing the circuit and lighting the LED.

---

## 4.3 Program Flowchart: Alternating LED Pattern (2 LEDs, 1 Hz)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start of Program]) --> Init[Port 1 configured as OUTPUT]
    Init --> Loop1[Set P1 to FEH LED0 ON LED1 OFF]
    Loop1 --> D1[Software Delay 500 ms]
    D1 --> Loop2[Set P1 to FDH LED0 OFF LED1 ON]
    Loop2 --> D2[Software Delay 500 ms]
    D2 --> Loop1
    Loop1 -.Repeated forever.-> Loop1
```

> *Explanation:* This flow models a **two-LED alternating blink** (a "ping-pong" pattern). The value `FEH = 11111110b` lights LED0, and `FDH = 11111101b` lights LED1. Each state is held for 500 ms, giving a complete 1-second cycle.

---

## 4.4 Component Pinout Reference (ASCII Schematic)

```
        +5V
         |
         |
        [R]  180 ohm  (¼ W)
         |
         |------>|+<|------ P1.0  (8051)
         |       LED            |
        GND                    GND (internal)
```

> *Explanation:* Even though the actual physical circuit uses **sinking topology** (LED on the high side), the ASCII shorthand shown above is a logical representation. In the sinking topology, $V_{CC}$ is on the top of the resistor, and the LED's cathode connects to the GPIO pin. The dot on the LED symbol represents the cathode (K).

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Verified ✅

| Rule | Compliance |
|---|---|
| All node IDs are purely alphanumeric | ✅ (Start, Init, Loop1, etc.) |
| No reserved keywords as standalone node IDs | ✅ |
| Node labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text | ✅ |
| No markdown bold or HTML tables inside node labels | ✅ |
| Mermaid block uses `flowchart TD` or `flowchart LR` | ✅ |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**"Why is a current-limiting resistor mandatory when interfacing an LED to a microcontroller pin? What happens if it is omitted?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A current-limiting resistor is mandatory because an LED is a **current-driven device**. Once the forward voltage ($V_F$) across the LED is reached, its internal resistance drops sharply, causing current to rise exponentially (as governed by the Shockley diode equation). Without a series resistor, the current would exceed the LED's maximum rating ($I_{F(max)} \approx 30$ mA), leading to **thermal runaway** and permanent destruction of the LED junction. Additionally, the resistor protects the microcontroller's GPIO pin from drawing excessive current that could damage the internal output driver transistor. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
**"Differentiate between current sourcing and current sinking with respect to 8051 GPIO pins. Which is preferred for LED interfacing and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Current Sourcing:** The GPIO pin acts as the current *source*, driving current OUT of the pin and into the load connected to ground. The 8051's source capability is limited to approximately **60 μA**, which is insufficient to drive a standard LED (requiring 10–20 mA). **(1 mark)**
- **Current Sinking:** The GPIO pin acts as the current *sink*, pulling current INTO the pin from a load connected to $V_{CC}$. The 8051's sink capability is up to **10 mA**, sufficient to drive a standard LED. **(1 mark)**
- **Preference:** **Current Sinking is preferred** for 8051 LED interfacing because the sink current rating is significantly higher than the source current rating, ensuring reliable LED illumination without overstressing the GPIO pin. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2, Q4(a) and Q4(b)]**

**Q4(a)** [7 Marks]: Design an interface circuit to connect **8 LEDs** to Port 1 of an 8051 microcontroller in current-sinking mode. Show the schematic and calculate the current-limiting resistor for a red LED ($V_F = 2.0\,V$, $I_F = 12\,mA$) assuming $V_{OL} = 0.4\,V$ and $V_{CC} = 5.0\,V$. Mention the standard resistor value and verify the actual current.

**Model Solution Q4(a) [7 Marks]:**

1. **Topology selection:** Current-sinking, since 8051 sinks better than it sources. LED anode connects to $V_{CC}$ via resistor; LED cathode connects to GPIO pin. **(1 mark)**

2. **KVL equation:** $V_{CC} = V_R + V_F + V_{OL}$ **(1 mark)**

3. **Calculation:**
   $$V_R = 5.0 - 2.0 - 0.4 = 2.6\,V$$
   $$R = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{2.6}{12 \times 10^{-3}} = 216.67\,\Omega$$ **(2 marks)**

4. **E12 selection:** Round up to next standard value → **220 Ω** **(1 mark)**

5. **Verification:** $I_{F(actual)} = \frac{2.6}{220} = 11.82\,mA$ (safely below 12 mA target) **(1 mark)**

6. **Power dissipation:** $P_R = (11.82 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 220 = 30.7\,mW$ → use **¼ W resistor** **(1 mark)**

---

**Q4(b)** [7 Marks]: Write an 8051 Embedded C program (using Keil C51) to **blink all 8 LEDs connected to Port 1** with a 1-second ON time and a 1-second OFF time, using a software delay loop.

**Model Solution Q4(b) [7 Marks]:**

```c
#include <reg51.h>

void delay(unsigned int time) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < time; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 1275; j++);   // calibrated for 1 ms per inner iteration
}

void main(void) {
    P1 = 0x00;             // Configure P1 as output (all LOW → all LEDs ON in sinking mode)
    while (1) {            // Infinite loop
        P1 = 0x00;         // All LEDs ON
        delay(1000);       // 1-second delay
        P1 = 0xFF;         // All LEDs OFF
        delay(1000);       // 1-second delay
    }
}
```

**Explanation (mark distribution):**
- Header inclusion & main function structure: **1 mark**
- `while(1)` infinite loop: **1 mark**
- LED ON logic (`P1 = 0x00` with explanation of sinking mode): **2 marks**
- LED OFF logic (`P1 = 0xFF`): **1 mark**
- `delay()` function with nested loops and 1-second calibration: **2 marks**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, Q5(a) and Q5(b)]**

**Q5(a)** [7 Marks]: With a neat diagram, explain the **current-sinking** method of interfacing an LED to an 8051 port pin. Why is the LED active when the pin is LOW? What is the role of the current-limiting resistor?

**Model Solution Q5(a) [7 Marks]:**

1. **Diagram** (ASCII + description): $V_{CC}$ → 180 Ω → LED anode → LED cathode → P1.0 (sink pin). **(2 marks)**

2. **Active-LOW explanation:** When P1.0 outputs **logic LOW (0)**, the internal N-channel MOSFET of the 8051 output stage turns ON, providing a low-resistance path to ground. Current flows from $V_{CC}$ → R → LED → pin → ground, illuminating the LED. When P1.0 is HIGH, both ends of the LED are at ~5V potential difference ≈ 0V, so no current flows and the LED is OFF. **(3 marks)**

3. **Resistor role:** Protects LED from excessive current (which would cause thermal destruction) and protects the GPIO pin from drawing more than its rated sink current. **(2 marks)**

---

**Q5(b)** [7 Marks]: Write an 8051 Assembly Language Program (ALP) to generate a **running light pattern** on 8 LEDs connected to Port 1. The pattern should shift a single glowing LED from P1.0 to P1.7 and then repeat. Use a software delay of approximately 200 ms between shifts.

**Model Solution Q5(b) [7 Marks]:**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        MOV P1, #0FFH      ; All LEDs OFF (sinking mode, all pins HIGH)
        MOV A, #0FEH       ; Initial pattern: 11111110b (only P1.0 LOW = LED0 ON)
        MOV R2, #08H       ; Counter for 8 LEDs

LOOP:   MOV P1, A          ; Output pattern to Port 1
        ACALL DELAY        ; 200 ms software delay
        RL A               ; Rotate accumulator LEFT to shift ON bit
        DJNZ R2, LOOP      ; Repeat 8 times
        SJMP 0000H         ; Restart pattern from beginning

DELAY:  MOV R1, #0FFH      ; Inner counter
D2:     MOV R0, #0C0H      ; Calibration for ~200 ms
D1:     DJNZ R0, D1
        DJNZ R1, D2
        RET

        END
```

**Explanation (mark distribution):**
- ALP header, ORG, END directives: **1 mark**
- Initial register setup (`A`, `R2`): **1 mark**
- Output to port and `RL A` rotate logic: **2 marks**
- DJNZ loop for 8 iterations: **1 mark**
- DELAY subroutine with double-nested loops: **2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LED Interfacing Questions (KTU Board Examiner Report):**
> 1. **Wrong topology assumption:** Many students assume sourcing mode is default and write "P1 = 1 → LED ON". For 8051, this draws only 60 μA — the LED will not visibly glow. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Missing $V_{OL}$ term:** In sinking-mode resistor calculation, students often write $R = (V_{CC} - V_F) / I_F$ and forget the $V_{OL} = 0.4V$ drop. This results in an incorrectly high resistor value and is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Not rounding UP to E12:** Selecting a value lower than the calculated resistance (e.g., choosing 150 Ω instead of 180 Ω) violates the safety principle. Examiners expect rounding UP.
> 4. **No power rating mentioned:** A common 1-mark deduction is failing to mention the resistor's wattage (¼ W or ½ W).
> 5. **Program without comments:** KTU valuation guidelines reward **comments and clarity**. A program with no comments or improper indentation loses up to 1 mark.
> 6. **Wrong delay calibration:** Claiming a 1-second delay without showing the calibration math (i.e., $f_{osc}$, machine cycles, loop counts) is treated as incomplete — typically a 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📋

### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ LED is a **current-driven, unidirectional** device with $V_F \approx 2V$ (red) and $I_F = 10$–$20$ mA.
- ✅ Always include a **current-limiting resistor (CLR)** in series — typical value: **180 Ω or 220 Ω** (E12 standard).
- ✅ **Sinking mode** is preferred for 8051: pin = **LOW (0)** → LED ON; pin = HIGH (1) → LED OFF.
- ✅ Formula for sinking-mode resistor: $R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F - V_{OL}}{I_F}$.
- ✅ Use **¼ watt (0.25 W)** resistors for standard LED currents (sufficient power headroom).
- ✅ **8051 crystal:** 11.0592 MHz is standard (also useful for UART baud rate generation); machine cycle = 12 / $f_{osc}$ ≈ 1.085 μs.
- ✅ Software delay: **double-nested DJNZ loop** with R0, R1 = FFH generates ~141 ms; call 7–8 times for ~1 s.
- ✅ Embedded C: `#include <reg51.h>`; configure port via `P1 = 0x00` or `P1 = 0xFF` based on topology.
- ✅ Assembly: `SETB P1.x` makes pin HIGH; `CLR P1.x` makes pin LOW.
- ✅ Power dissipation check: $P_R = I_F^2 \cdot R$ — must be < resistor wattage rating.

### 🎯 Examiner-Expectation Summary

For a full 14-mark answer, always provide:
1. **Schematic / connection diagram** (2 marks)
2. **Calculations** with proper units (3 marks)
3. **Topology justification** (sinking vs sourcing) (2 marks)
4. **Complete program** in C or Assembly with comments (5 marks)
5. **Verification and conclusion** (1 mark)
6. **Real-world application mention** (1 mark — bonus credibility)

### 🧠 Key Mnemonic
> **"SINK to LIGHT"** — *Sinking mode makes the LED bright.*
> **"HIGH is OFF, LOW is ON"** — *in sinking mode.*

---

*End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Interfacing LED*
*Prepared in alignment with KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) assessment regulations.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing LCD Displays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL507 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Interfacing LCD Displays ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **LCD Interfacing** refers to the systematic hardware and software integration of a **Liquid Crystal Display (LCD)** module with a microcontroller (typically the **8051 family** or **ARM Cortex-M** in KTU labs) using parallel communication protocols, governed by the **Hitachi HD44780** controller standard. The interface enables the microcontroller to display alphanumeric characters, custom symbols, and real-time system variables on a visual output device. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus (PCICL507):** The laboratory examination evaluates the student's ability to (a) draw the interfacing circuit, (b) write the initialization sequence, (c) generate correct timing/control waveforms, and (d) code in Embedded C/Assembly to display strings and generate custom characters. ### 1.2 The Standard Module: 16×2 Character LCD The most commonly interfaced LCD in KTU labs is the **JHD162A / LM016L 16×2 LCD**: - **16 columns × 2 rows** = 32 character cells - Each character is a **5×8 dot matrix** (5×10 optional) - Built-in **HD44780 controller** with **Character Generator ROM (CGROM)** for ASCII - **Character Generator RAM (CGRAM)** — 64 bytes (8 custom characters max) - **Display Data RAM (DDRAM)** — 80 bytes (40 per line) ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Notice Board" Model > [!NOTE] > **Plain English Analogy:** Think of the LCD as a **digital notice board in a classroom**. The microcontroller is the **teacher** who decides what to write. The teacher cannot just shout messages — the board needs **specific instructions** (write/erase), **ink** (data), and a **gatekeeper** to know when the message is fully received (Enable pulse). The HD44780 controller is the **office clerk** sitting next to the board who manages all the internal arrangements so the teacher only sends clean, simple commands. ### 1.4 Physical Pin Map of the 16×2 LCD | Pin # | Symbol | Type | Function | |-------|--------|------|----------| | 1 | **VSS** | Power | Ground (**0 V**) | | 2 | **VDD** | Power | Logic Supply (**+5 V**) | | 3 | **VEE / Vo** | Analog | Contrast adjustment (via potentiometer) | | 4 | **RS** | Input | **Register Select** (0 = Command, 1 = Data) | | 5 | **R/W** | Input | Read/Write (0 = Write, 1 = Read) | | 6 | **E** | Input | **Enable** — data latched on **HIGH-to-LOW** transition | | 7–14 | **D0–D7** | Bidirectional | 8-bit data bus (or 4-bit mode uses D4–D7) | | 15 | **A / LED+** | Power | Backlight anode (**+5 V**) | | 16 | **K / LED–** | Power | Backlight cathode (**GND**) | ### 1.5 Operational Modes - **8-bit Mode:** All 8 data lines (D0–D7) used → faster, more pins consumed. - **4-bit Mode:** Only D4–D7 used → saves microcontroller pins, two nibbles sent per byte (most popular in KTU labs). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Lab Standard:** 8051-based experiments commonly use **8-bit mode** (P0 as data, P2 as control). ARM-based labs frequently use **4-bit mode** to preserve GPIO. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step The HD44780 operates as a **stateful peripheral**. Every transaction follows this strict sequence: 1. **Power-On Reset** — Internal reset circuit holds the controller for **~15 ms** after VDD reaches 4.5 V. 2. **Initialization Sequence** — Software sends a fixed command stream to configure the controller (Function Set, Display ON/OFF, Clear Display, Entry Mode). 3. **Command Mode (RS = 0)** — Microcontroller issues configuration commands (cursor, clear, shift). 4. **Data Mode (RS = 1)** — Microcontroller writes ASCII codes for characters to be displayed. 5. **Enable Pulse (E)** — A HIGH-to-LOW transition of the Enable line **latches** the data/command into the LCD. The pulse width must be **≥ 450 ns**. ### 2.2 The RS, R/W, E Control Truth Table | RS | R/W | E (falling edge) | Operation | |----|-----|------------------|-----------| | 0 | 0 | ↓ | **Write Command** (clear, cursor, function set) | | 0 | 1 | ↓ | **Read Busy Flag + Address Counter** | | 1 | 0 | ↓ | **Write Data** (ASCII character to DDRAM/CGRAM) | | 1 | 1 | ↓ | **Read Data** from DDRAM/CGRAM | ### 2.3 Address Map of DDRAM (16×2 LCD) | Line | Address Range (Hex) | First Address | |------|---------------------|---------------| | Line 1 | **0x80 – 0x8F** | 0x80 | | Line 2 | **0xC0 – 0xCF** | 0xC0 | > [!NOTE] > Setting DDRAM address is done via the **"Set DDRAM Address" command** = `1AAAAAAAh` (binary), where the 7 A-bits form the address. Example: To start at Line 2 column 1, send `0xC0` as a command. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Command Cheat Sheet | Parameter / Command | Value (Hex) | Description | |----------------------|-------------|-------------| | **Function Set (8-bit, 2-line, 5×8)** | `0x38` | Selects 8-bit interface, 2 display lines | | **Function Set (4-bit, 2-line, 5×8)** | `0x28` | Selects 4-bit interface, 2 display lines | | **Display ON, Cursor OFF** | `0x0C` | Display enabled, cursor hidden | | **Display ON, Cursor ON, Blink** | `0x0F` | Display + cursor + blinking block | | **Display OFF** | `0x08` | Turns display off (data retained) | | **Clear Display** | `0x01` | Clears DDRAM, address → 0x00, ~1.53 ms delay | | **Entry Mode Set (Increment, No Shift)** | `0x06` | Auto-increment cursor after each write | | **Entry Mode Set (Increment, Shift)** | `0x07` | Auto-increment + shift entire display | | **Shift Cursor Left** | `0x10` | Moves cursor one position left | | **Shift Cursor Right** | `0x14` | Moves cursor one position right | | **Shift Entire Display Left** | `0x18` | Right-to-left scroll | | **Shift Entire Display Right** | `0x1C` | Left-to-right scroll | | **Set DDRAM Address** | `0x80 + addr` | Positions cursor | | **Set CGRAM Address** | `0x40 + addr` | For custom character generation | ### 2.5 Timing Parameters (Critical for KTU Lab Viva) | Timing Parameter | Symbol | Minimum Value | Typical | |------------------|--------|---------------|---------| | Enable Pulse Width (HIGH) | **tPW** | **450 ns** | 1 µs | | Enable Cycle Time | **tC** | **1 µs** | — | | Data Setup Time | **tDSW** | **195 ns** | — | | Data Hold Time | **tH** | **10 ns** | — | | Command Execution Time (most) | — | **40 µs** | 37 µs | | Clear Display Command | — | **1.53 ms** | — | | Address Set Time | — | **40 µs** | — | | Power-On Reset | **tR** | **15 ms** | 30 ms | ### 2.6 Engineering Utility of LCD Interfacing - **Industrial HMI:** Machine status, fault codes, RPM readouts. - **Medical Devices:** Patient monitors, infusion pumps. - **Consumer Electronics:** Microwave ovens, washing machines, weigh scales. - **Automotive:** Dashboard indicators, climate control panels. - **IoT Edge Nodes:** Local debugging interface without PC dependency. ### 2.7 Interfacing Mathematical Formulas The total number of **distinct characters** displayable on a 16×2 LCD is: $$N_{\text{chars}} = R \times C = 2 \times 16 = 32 \text{ characters}$$ The total **dot matrix pixels** actively lit is: $$N_{\text{pixels}} = N_{\text{chars}} \times 5 \times 8 = 32 \times 40 = 1280 \text{ pixels}$$ Time to refresh the full display in 8-bit mode (one enable cycle per byte): $$T_{\text{full}} = 32 \times t_C = 32 \times 1\ \mu s = 32\ \mu s$$ The CGRAM custom character generation bit-mask formula for row $r$ of an 8-row custom glyph: $$B_r = \sum_{c=1}^{5} b_{r,c} \cdot 2^{(5-c)}, \quad b_{r,c} \in \{0,1\}$$ ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Programs & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Custom Character (Smiley Face) — Bit Map Derivation Let us derive the CGRAM byte values for a **smiley face** custom character on a 5×8 dot matrix. The HD44780 stores each row as a **5-bit value** (LSB = leftmost pixel), padded with three zeros in the upper 3 bits. **Target Visual:** ``` Row 0: 0 0 0 0 0 → empty (top margin) Row 1: 0 0 0 0 0 → empty Row 2: 0 0 1 0 0 → nose tip Row 3: 0 1 0 1 0 → eyes Row 4: 0 1 0 1 0 → eyes Row 5: 1 1 1 1 1 → mouth Row 6: 0 1 1 1 0 → mouth curve Row 7: 0 0 0 0 0 → empty ``` > **Step 1 — Compute each row using $B_r = \sum b_{r,c} \cdot 2^{(5-c)}$** For Row 2 (0 0 1 0 0) — left to right = $b_{r,1}b_{r,2}b_{r,3}b_{r,4}b_{r,5}$: $$ B_2 = (0)(16) + (0)(8) + (1)(4) + (0)(2) + (0)(1) = 4 $$ For Row 3 (0 1 0 1 0): $$ B_3 = (0)(16) + (1)(8) + (0)(4) + (1)(2) + (0)(1) = 10 $$ For Row 5 (1 1 1 1 1): $$ B_5 = (1)(16) + (1)(8) + (1)(4) + (1)(2) + (1)(1) = 31 $$ For Row 6 (0 1 1 1 0): $$ B_6 = (0)(16) + (1)(8) + (1)(4) + (1)(2) + (0)(1) = 14 $$ > **Step 2 — Full CGRAM byte array (8 rows, indexed 0x40 to 0x47)** $$ \text{CGRAM} = \{0x00,\ 0x00,\ 0x04,\ 0x0A,\ 0x0A,\ 0x1F,\ 0x0E,\ 0x00\} $$ > **Step 3 — Access character** — Set CGRAM address `0x40`, then write the 8 bytes. The character is now referenced by **ASCII 0x00** when written to DDRAM. ### 3.2 Complete 8051 Embedded C Program (8-bit Mode) ```c #include sbit RS = P2^0; // Register Select sbit RW = P2^1; // Read/Write sbit EN = P2^2; // Enable void lcd_delay(unsigned int ms) { unsigned int i, j; for(i = 0; i < ms; i++) for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++); } void lcd_cmd(unsigned char cmd) { P0 = cmd; // Put command on data bus RS = 0; // Command mode RW = 0; // Write operation EN = 1; // Enable HIGH lcd_delay(1); EN = 0; // HIGH-to-LOW latch lcd_delay(2); } void lcd_data(unsigned char dat) { P0 = dat; // Put data on bus RS = 1; // Data mode RW = 0; // Write EN = 1; lcd_delay(1); EN = 0; lcd_delay(2); } void lcd_init(void) { lcd_delay(20); // Power-on delay (>15 ms) lcd_cmd(0x38); // 8-bit, 2-line, 5x8 lcd_cmd(0x0C); // Display ON, cursor OFF lcd_cmd(0x01); // Clear display lcd_delay(2); // Wait >1.53 ms lcd_cmd(0x06); // Entry mode: increment } void lcd_string(unsigned char *str) { while(*str) lcd_data(*str++); } void main(void) { unsigned char smile[8] = {0x00,0x00,0x04,0x0A,0x0A,0x1F,0x0E,0x00}; unsigned char i; lcd_init(); // Line 1 lcd_cmd(0x80); // Cursor at line 1, col 0 lcd_string("KTU 2024 SCHEME"); // Line 2 lcd_cmd(0xC0); // Cursor at line 2, col 0 lcd_string("LCD LAB OK "); // Custom smiley at end of line 2 lcd_cmd(0x48); // CGRAM address = 0x40 for(i = 0; i < 8; i++) lcd_data(smile[i]); lcd_cmd(0xC9); // Position cursor lcd_data(0x00); // Display custom char 0 while(1); } ``` ### 3.3 Initialization Sequence — Analytical Breakdown > [!IMPORTANT] > The HD44780 **automatically resets** to 8-bit mode on power-up. Therefore, a software-initiated 4-bit mode requires sending the `0x30` function-set command **three times** as a "wake-up" call before the controller accepts the 4-bit switch `0x20`. For **8-bit mode** (used in the program above), the initialization sequence is: $$ \text{Power On} \xrightarrow{\text{Wait 15 ms}} 0x38 \xrightarrow{40\ \mu s} 0x0C \xrightarrow{40\ \mu s} 0x01 \xrightarrow{1.53\ ms} 0x06 $$ ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | **8-bit Mode** | **4-bit Mode** | |---------|---------------|----------------| | Data lines used | 8 (D0–D7) | 4 (D4–D7) | | Throughput | 1 nibble cycle = 1 byte | 2 nibble cycles = 1 byte (slower) | | MCU pins saved | 0 | **4 pins** | | Code complexity | Simpler | More complex (nibble sequencing) | | KTU 8051 lab preference | **High** | Low | | KTU ARM Cortex lab preference | Medium | **High** | | Initial command count | 4 commands | 7+ commands | | Busy flag read | Single read | Requires 2 reads + combine | ### 3.5 Busy Flag Polling vs. Delay Loops $$T_{\text{delay\_total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n_{\text{cmds}}} t_{\text{exec},i} = (n-1) \times 40\ \mu s + 1530\ \mu s$$ For typical display sequence with 1 clear + 3 normal commands: $$ T_{\text{delay\_total}} = 3 \times 40\ \mu s + 1530\ \mu s = 1650\ \mu s $$ In **busy-flag polling**, the MCU reads D7 (BF bit) and waits until BF = 0, saving ~80% of waiting time. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Interfacing Architecture (8051 ↔ LCD) ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["8051 Microcontroller
P0 Data Bus
P2 Control Lines"] CTRL["Control Logic
RS, R/W, EN"] LCDC["HD44780 Controller
DDRAM, CGROM, CGRAM"] DRV["Dot Matrix Driver
40 Segment x 16 Common"] LCD["16x2 LCD Glass
Visible Character Cells"] MCU -->|8-bit Parallel| CTRL CTRL -->|Command or Data| LCDC LCDC -->|Pixel Data| DRV DRV -->|Multiplexed Voltages| LCD ``` ### 4.2 Detailed Pin Interconnection Map (8051 Standard) ```mermaid flowchart TB VCC["+5V Supply"] GND["Ground 0V"] POT["10K Potentiometer
Contrast Control"] BL["Backlight LED+5V"] BLC["Backlight LED GND"] subgraph MCU["8051 Microcontroller"] P0["P0.0 - P0.7
Data Bus D0-D7"] P20["P2.0 = RS"] P21["P2.1 = RW"] P22["P2.2 = EN"] end subgraph LCD["16x2 LCD Module"] PIN1["Pin 1 VSS"] PIN2["Pin 2 VDD"] PIN3["Pin 3 Vo"] PIN4["Pin 4 RS"] PIN5["Pin 5 RW"] PIN6["Pin 6 E"] PIN7TO14["Pin 7-14 D0-D7"] PIN15["Pin 15 A"] PIN16["Pin 16 K"] end VCC --> PIN2 GND --> PIN1 VCC --> POT POT --> PIN3 GND --> POT PIN4 --- P20 PIN5 --- P21 PIN6 --- P22 PIN7TO14 --- P0 VCC --> BL BL --> PIN15 PIN16 --- BLC ``` ### 4.3 LCD Write Operation Timing Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant MCU as Microcontroller participant LCD as HD44780 LCD Note over MCU,LCD: Step 1: Place Data/Command on Bus MCU->>LCD: D0-D7 = valid byte Note over MCU,LCD: Step 2: Set Control Lines MCU->>LCD: RS = 0 (Command) or 1 (Data) MCU->>LCD: RW = 0 (Write) Note over MCU,LCD: Step 3: Generate Enable Pulse MCU->>LCD: E = 1 (wait >= 450 ns) MCU->>LCD: E = 0 (falling edge latches) Note over LCD: Internal processing (~40 us) LCD-->>MCU: Ready for next operation ``` ### 4.4 Software State Machine for LCD Interface ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> PowerOn PowerOn --> Wait15ms: VDD reaches 4.5V Wait15ms --> FunctionSet: Send 0x38 FunctionSet --> DisplayControl: Send 0x0C DisplayControl --> ClearDisplay: Send 0x01 ClearDisplay --> Wait2ms: Delay 1.53ms Wait2ms --> EntryMode: Send 0x06 EntryMode --> ReadyState: Initialization Complete ReadyState --> WriteCommand: RS=0, RW=0 ReadyState --> WriteData: RS=1, RW=0 WriteCommand --> ReadyState WriteData --> ReadyState ReadyState --> BusyCheck: Read BF BusyCheck --> ReadyState: BF=0 BusyCheck --> BusyWait: BF=1 BusyWait --> BusyCheck ``` ### 4.5 Command-Data Processing Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD START([User Calls lcd_write]) --> SETBUS{Is it Data?} SETBUS -->|Yes Command| RSLOW["RS = 0"] SETBUS -->|No Data| RSHIGH["RS = 1"] RSLOW --> RWLOW["RW = 0"] RSHIGH --> RWLOW RWLOW --> PUTDATA["P0 = byte"] PUTDATA --> EHIGH["E = 1"] EHIGH --> DELAY1["Delay 1 us"] DELAY1 --> ELOW["E = 0 (Latch)"] ELOW --> DELAY2["Delay 40 us or Poll BF"] DELAY2 --> DONE([Return to Caller]) ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** **Explain the function of the RS, R/W, and Enable pins of a 16×2 LCD module interfaced with a microcontroller.** **Model Answer:** The **16×2 LCD** has three critical control pins managed by the microcontroller: - **RS (Register Select):** When **RS = 0**, the LCD interprets the byte on the data bus as a **command** (clear display, cursor position, function set). When **RS = 1**, it is treated as **data** (an ASCII character to display). - **R/W (Read/Write):** When **R/W = 0**, the LCD accepts a **write** operation from the MCU. When **R/W = 1**, the MCU can read the **Busy Flag (D7)** or the current DDRAM contents. - **E (Enable):** A **HIGH-to-LOW transition** of the Enable pin latches the data/command on the bus into the LCD's internal register. The pulse HIGH width must be **≥ 450 ns**, with a minimum cycle time of **1 µs**. --- #### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** **What is the function of the HD44780 controller? List the three memory blocks it contains.** **Model Answer:** The **HD44780** is a **dot-matrix LCD controller/driver** manufactured by Hitachi. It serves as the embedded "brain" inside 16×2 character LCD modules, offloading all pixel-level multiplexing, timing, and refresh tasks from the microcontroller. It contains three memory blocks: 1. **DDRAM (Display Data RAM)** — 80 bytes, holds the ASCII codes of characters currently displayed. Addresses `0x00–0x0F` map to Line 1; `0x40–0x4F` map to Line 2. 2. **CGROM (Character Generator ROM)** — Permanent storage for the standard **5×8 dot patterns** of ASCII characters (A–Z, 0–9, punctuation, Japanese kana). 3. **CGRAM (Character Generator RAM)** — 64 bytes of volatile RAM (8 custom characters × 8 bytes each), allowing the user to define **up to 8 unique 5×8 symbols**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]** **(a)** Draw the interfacing circuit diagram of an **8051 microcontroller** with a **16×2 LCD in 8-bit mode** using Port 0 as the data bus and Port 2 pins for control. Label all connections. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write an Embedded C program for 8051 to display the string **"KTU LAB"** on the first line and the string **"LCD TEST"** on the second line of the LCD. Include initialization. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Circuit Diagram:** ``` 8051 Microcontroller ┌──────────────┐ P0.0-P0.7├──────┐ │ ┌────────────────────┐ │ │ │ │ 16x2 LCD Module │ P2.0 ────┼──┐ │ │ │ │ P2.1 ────┼──┼───┼───────┼──────┤ RS (Pin 4) │ P2.2 ────┼──┼───┼───────┼──────┤ RW (Pin 5) │ │ │ │ └──────┤ E (Pin 6) │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ┌───────┤ D0-D7 (Pins 7-14) │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ ┌────┤ VSS (Pin 1) = GND │ │ │ │ │ ├────┤ VDD (Pin 2) = +5V │ │ │ │ │ │ ┌─┤ VEE (Pin 3) ← 10K│ │ │ │ │ │ │ │ Pot │ │ │ │ │ │ │ └────────────────────┘ └──┴───┴──────┴──┴──┘ ``` **Pin Connections Table:** | 8051 Pin | LCD Pin | Function | |----------|---------|----------| | P0.0–P0.7 | D0–D7 | 8-bit data bus | | P2.0 | Pin 4 (RS) | Register Select | | P2.1 | Pin 5 (R/W) | Read/Write | | P2.2 | Pin 6 (E) | Enable | | VCC | Pin 2 (VDD) | +5 V Logic Power | | VCC | Pin 15 (A) | Backlight + | | GND | Pin 1 (VSS) | Ground | | GND | Pin 16 (K) | Backlight – | | 10K Pot wiper | Pin 3 (Vo) | Contrast | **(b) Embedded C Program:** ```c #include sbit RS = P2^0; sbit RW = P2^1; sbit EN = P2^2; void delay(unsigned int ms) { unsigned int i, j; for(i = 0; i < ms; i++) for(j = 0; j < 1275; j++); } void lcd_cmd(unsigned char cmd) { P0 = cmd; RS = 0; RW = 0; EN = 1; delay(1); EN = 0; delay(2); } void lcd_data(unsigned char dat) { P0 = dat; RS = 1; RW = 0; EN = 1; delay(1); EN = 0; delay(2); } void lcd_string(unsigned char *s) { while(*s) lcd_data(*s++); } void main(void) { delay(20); lcd_cmd(0x38); // 8-bit, 2-line, 5x8 lcd_cmd(0x0C); // Display ON, cursor OFF lcd_cmd(0x01); // Clear display delay(2); lcd_cmd(0x06); // Entry mode increment lcd_cmd(0x80); // Line 1 start lcd_string("KTU LAB");
