When the MCU pin is LOW (0 V), the LED turns ON. **Step 2 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop.** $$V_{cc} - V_{R} - V_F - V_{OL} = 0$$ where $V_{OL} \approx 0.5\ \text{V}$ (worst-case ATmega328P output LOW voltage at 12 mA). **Step 3 — Solve for the resistor voltage drop.** $$V_{R} = V_{cc} - V_F - V_{OL} = 5.0 - 2.0 - 0.5 = 2.5\ \text{V}$$ **Step 4 — Apply Ohm's Law to find R.** $$R = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{2.5\ \text{V}}{12\ \text{mA}} = \frac{2.5}{0.012} = 208.33\ \Omega$$ **Step 5 — Select the nearest standard E12 resistor.** The closest standard value is **220 Ω**. **Step 6 — Verify the new actual current.** $$I_{F,actual} = \frac{2.5}{220} = 11.36\ \text{mA}$$ This is within ±5% of the design target — acceptable. **Step 7 — Calculate total current for 4 LEDs.** $$I_{total} = 4 \times 11.36\ \text{mA} = 45.45\ \text{mA}$$ This is **well below** the 200 mA absolute maximum of the entire ATmega328P package, and well within each pin's 40 mA limit. ✅ **Step 8 — Calculate power dissipation.** Per LED: $$P_{LED} = V_F \times I_{F,actual} = 2.0 \times 0.01136 = 0.0227\ \text{W} = 22.7\ \text{mW}$$ Per resistor: $$P_{R} = I_{F,actual}^2 \times R = (0.01136)^2 \times 220 = 0.0284\ \text{W} = 28.4\ \text{mW}$$ A standard **¼ W (250 mW)** resistor is more than sufficient. ✅ **Step 9 — Write the firmware (Arduino-C).** ```c // ATmega328P - 4 LEDs on PB0..PB3 (Arduino UNO digital pins 8..11) void setup() { for (int pin = 8; pin <= 11; pin++) { pinMode(pin, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(pin, LOW); // Active-low: LOW = LED ON } } void loop() { for (int pin = 8; pin <= 11; pin++) { digitalWrite(pin, LOW); // LED ON delay(300); digitalWrite(pin, HIGH); // LED OFF delay(300); } } ``` ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: LM35 Temperature to Digital Conversion ### Problem Statement *"An LM35 temperature sensor is connected to ADC channel ADC0 (pin A0) of an Arduino UNO (5 V reference, 10-bit ADC). The ADC reads a value of **204**. Calculate the actual temperature in °C and the equivalent voltage at the sensor output."* ### Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1 — Recall the LM35 transfer function.** $$V_{out} = 0.010\ \text{V/°C} \times T \quad \text{or} \quad T = \frac{V_{out}}{0.010}$$ **Step 2 — Convert the raw ADC value to voltage.** $$V_{out} = \frac{ADC}{1023} \times V_{ref} = \frac{204}{1023} \times 5.0$$ Let's compute this carefully: $$V_{out} = \frac{204 \times 5.0}{1023} = \frac{1020.0}{1023} = 0.99707\ \text{V}$$ **Step 3 — Convert voltage to temperature.** $$T = \frac{V_{out}}{0.010} = \frac{0.99707}{0.010} = 99.707\ ^\circ \text{C}$$ **Step 4 — Cross-check using the shortcut formula.** $$T(^\circ \text{C}) = \frac{ADC \times V_{ref}}{1023 \times 0.010} = \frac{204 \times 5.0}{1023 \times 0.010}$$ $$T = \frac{1020.0}{10.23} = 99.707\ ^\circ \text{C} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 — Firmware representation.** ```c int raw = analogRead(A0); float voltage = raw * (5.0 / 1023.0); // Convert to voltage float tempC = voltage / 0.010; // LM35: 10 mV per °C float tempF = tempC * 9.0/5.0 + 32.0; // Optional: Fahrenheit ``` > [!NOTE] > **Smoothing (averaging) tip:** Real LM35 readings are noisy. Use a **moving average** of 16–64 samples for stable lab display: > ```c > const int N = 32; > long sum = 0; > for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) sum += analogRead(A0); > float avgADC = sum / (float)N; > ``` ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Switch Debounce Timing **Problem:** *"A tactile push-button has a mechanical bounce time of 12 ms. The MCU polls every 5 ms. Determine the minimum debounce delay $T_D$ needed to ensure reliable single-event detection, and the maximum safe polling frequency."* **Step 1 — The debounce delay must exceed the worst-case bounce duration.** $$T_D \geq T_{bounce,max} = 12\ \text{ms}$$ Following KTU convention, we add a **50% safety margin**: $$T_D = 1.5 \times 12\ \text{ms} = 18\ \text{ms}$$ Round up to the next convenient value: **20 ms** (or 50 ms for high-reliability applications). **Step 2 — Maximum safe polling frequency.** The polling period must be longer than the debounce delay to avoid double-counting: $$T_{poll} \geq T_D \implies f_{poll,max} = \frac{1}{T_D} = \frac{1}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 50\ \text{Hz}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitfall:** Polling faster than 50 Hz with $T_D = 20\ \text{ms}$ can cause the **same** press to be detected **twice**. This is the most common KTU lab viva question. ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Feature | LED Interface | Switch Interface | Analog Sensor | Digital Sensor | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Signal direction** | MCU → LED | Button → MCU | Sensor → ADC | Sensor → MCU | | **Pin mode** | OUTPUT | INPUT_PULLUP | INPUT (analog) | INPUT (digital) | | **Signal type** | Digital (sink/source) | Digital (debounced) | Continuous voltage | Bit-serial protocol | | **Hardware extra** | Resistor R | Pull-up R | Reference, bypass cap | Pull-up, level shifter | | **Typical library/code** | `digitalWrite()` | `digitalRead()` | `analogRead()` | `dht.read()` etc. | | **Time-critical?** | No | Yes (debounce) | No | Yes (protocol timing) | | **Power level** | 5–20 mA | < 1 mA | < 1 mA (passive) | 1–100 mA (active) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Interfacing Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[MICROCONTROLLER
ATmega328P / 8051 / MSP430]:::mcu B[PORT REGISTERS
DDRx PORTx PINx]:::reg C[POWER STAGE
Resistor / Driver]:::power D[ACTUATORS
LEDs Buzzers Relays]:::out E[INPUT SOURCES
Switches Buttons]:::in F[ADC CHANNELS
SAR 10-bit]:::adc G[ANALOG SENSORS
LM35 LDR Pot]:::analog H[DIGITAL SENSORS
DHT11 PIR DS18B20]:::dig I[USER INTERFACE
LCD / Serial Monitor]:::ui A --> B B -->|Output| C --> D E -->|Debounced| B G --> F --> B H -->|Protocol| B B -->|Display| I classDef mcu fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef reg fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef power fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef in fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#800080,color:#000 classDef adc fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000 classDef analog fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef dig fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef ui fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Circuit Schematic — LED + Switch + LM35 Combined (Mermaid Fallback) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MCU_BLOCK [ATMEGA328P MICROCONTROLLER] PB0[PB0 / Pin 8 - LED1] PB1[PB1 / Pin 9 - LED2] PC0[PC0 / ADC0 - LM35] PD2[PD2 / Pin 2 - SW1] end subgraph LED_DRIVER [LED DRIVER STAGE] R1[R1 = 330 ohm] R2[R2 = 330 ohm] LED1[D1 RED LED] LED2[D2 GREEN LED] end subgraph SW_INPUT [SWITCH INPUT STAGE] R3[R_pullup = 10k ohm to Vcc] SW1[Tactile Switch to GND] end subgraph SENSOR_INPUT [ANALOG SENSOR STAGE] LM35[LM35 Temperature Sensor] C1[100 nF Decoupling Cap] end VCC[+5V Supply]:::power GND[Ground 0V]:::gnd VCC --> R1 --> LED1 --> PB0 VCC --> R2 --> LED2 --> PB1 VCC --> R3 R3 --> PD2 SW1 --> PD2 SW1 --> GND VCC --> LM35 LM35 --> C1 LM35 --> PC0 LM35 --> GND PB0 --> GND PB1 --> GND classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef gnd fill:#000,stroke:#000,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.3 Program Flowchart — Polling-Based Sensor-Actuator Control ```mermaid flowchart TD A[START] --> B[Initialize GPIO
DDRx = OUTPUT/INPUT] B --> C[Enable Pull-ups
and ADC] C --> D[Read Switch State
digitalRead] D --> E{Is Switch
Pressed?} E -->|NO| F[All LEDs OFF] E -->|YES| G[Read Analog Sensor
analogRead] G --> H[Convert ADC to
Engineering Units] H --> I{Sensor Value
> Threshold?} I -->|YES| J[Turn ON Red LED
Buzzer Alert] I -->|NO| K[Turn ON Green LED
Normal State] J --> L[Display on
LCD/Serial] K --> L L --> M[Debounce Delay
50 ms] F --> M M --> D ``` ## 4.4 Register-Level Architecture (AVR) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[CPU Core
ALU + Registers]:::core B[DDRB
Direction Register]:::reg C[PORTB
Output Latch]:::reg D[PINB
Input Pin Register]:::reg E[Physical Pins
PB0..PB7]:::pin F[ADCSRA
ADC Control]:::adc G[ADMUX
Channel Select]:::adc H[ADC Data
Registers ADCH ADCL]:::adc A -->|Write 1| B B -->|Configures| E A -->|Write Data| C C -->|Drives when OUTPUT| E E -->|Sensed Voltage| D D -->|Read by| A A -->|Start conversion| F F -->|Selects channel| G G -->|Returns| H H -->|Read by| A classDef core fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef reg fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef pin fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef adc fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#808000,color:#000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Explain the concept of a "current-limiting resistor" in LED interfacing with a microcontroller. Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A current-limiting resistor is connected in series with an LED to restrict the current flowing through it to a safe value. LEDs are **non-linear devices** — once the forward voltage $V_F$ is exceeded, current rises sharply, potentially destroying the diode and damaging the MCU pin. The resistor value is calculated using **KVL**: $R = (V_{cc} - V_F) / I_F$. For a typical red LED ($V_F = 2\ \text{V}$, $I_F = 10\ \text{mA}$, $V_{cc} = 5\ \text{V}$), $R = 300\ \Omega \approx 330\ \Omega$ standard value. It is **mandatory** to protect both the LED and the microcontroller I/O pin from exceeding the absolute maximum current rating of **40 mA** per pin (ATmega328P). ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is switch debouncing? List two methods to implement it.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Switch debouncing is the technique used to eliminate the spurious multiple transitions caused by the mechanical bounce of switch contacts, which typically lasts **5 to 20 ms**. **Two methods:** 1. **Hardware debouncing:** Using an **RC low-pass filter** (e.g., $R = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$, $C = 1\ \mu\text{F}$ → $T_{RC} = 10\ \text{ms}$) followed by a **Schmitt trigger** (e.g., 74HC14) to produce clean edges. 2. **Software debouncing:** Using a **time-stamped delay** in firmware. After detecting a change in input, wait for at least **20–50 ms** before accepting the new state, ensuring all bouncing has settled. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice ### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** *[7 marks]* Design an interface circuit to connect **8 LEDs** to **PORTB** of an ATmega328P (Arduino UNO) such that they form a **running-light (chasing) pattern**. Draw the schematic, state the current-limiting resistor calculation, and write the complete Arduino sketch. **(b)** *[7 marks]* With the same circuit, modify the program so that the chasing direction is controlled by **two tactile push-buttons** connected to **PD2 and PD3** — one for "left chase" and one for "right chase". Show the debounce logic and explain the active-low wiring. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(a) **Hardware Design:** - 8 LEDs connected in **current-sinking** mode: Anode → 330 Ω → +5 V, Cathode → PORTB pin. - Resistor calculation: $R = (5 - 2) / 0.010 = 300\ \Omega \approx 330\ \Omega$. - Total current when all 8 LEDs ON: $8 \times 10\ \text{mA} = 80\ \text{mA}$ (within 200 mA budget). **Complete Arduino Sketch:** ```c // Running light on PORTB (Arduino digital pins 8..15) // Note: Arduino UNO has pins 8..13 on PORTB; for 8 LEDs use 8..13 + A0,A1 // or use a shift register. For simplicity, we use pins 2..9 here. int ledPins[] = {2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9}; int ledCount = 8; void setup() { for (int i = 0; i < ledCount; i++) { pinMode(ledPins[i], OUTPUT); digitalWrite(ledPins[i], LOW); // active-low: LOW = ON } } void loop() { // Forward chase for (int i = 0; i < ledCount; i++) { digitalWrite(ledPins[i], HIGH); // OFF } for (int i = 0; i < ledCount; i++) { digitalWrite(ledPins[i], LOW); // ON delay(150); digitalWrite(ledPins[i], HIGH); // OFF } // Reverse chase for (int i = ledCount - 1; i >= 0; i--) { digitalWrite(ledPins[i], LOW); delay(150); digitalWrite(ledPins[i], HIGH); } } ``` --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-A(b) **Hardware Addition:** - SW1 (Left) → PD2 with **10 kΩ pull-up to Vcc** → other terminal to GND. - SW2 (Right) → PD3 with **10 kΩ pull-up to Vcc** → other terminal to GND. - Both buttons are **active-low** (pressed = 0 V, released = 5 V). - **Internal pull-ups** of ATmega328P can replace external 10 kΩ resistors (`pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP)`). **Complete Firmware:** ```c const int ledPins[] = {2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9}; const int ledCount = 8; const int btnLeft = 10; // PD2 = Arduino pin 10 (example) const int btnRight = 11; // PD3 = Arduino pin 11 (example) unsigned long lastDebounceLeft = 0, lastDebounceRight = 0; const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 30; bool leftState = false, rightState = false; void setup() { for (int i = 0; i < ledCount; i++) pinMode(ledPins[i], OUTPUT); pinMode(btnLeft, INPUT_PULLUP); pinMode(btnRight, INPUT_PULLUP); Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { bool leftNow = (digitalRead(btnLeft) == LOW); bool rightNow = (digitalRead(btnRight) == LOW); if (leftNow && millis() - lastDebounceLeft > DEBOUNCE_MS) { leftState = true; rightState = false; lastDebounceLeft = millis(); } if (rightNow && millis() - lastDebounceRight > DEBOUNCE_MS) { rightState = true; leftState = false; lastDebounceRight = millis(); } static int pos = 0; if (leftState) pos = (pos + 1) % ledCount; // forward if (rightState) pos = (pos - 1 + ledCount) % ledCount; // reverse for (int i = 0; i < ledCount; i++) digitalWrite(ledPins[i], HIGH); digitalWrite(ledPins[pos], LOW); delay(100); } ``` **Explanation of active-low wiring:** Because the switches connect the MCU pin to **GND when pressed**, a pull-up resistor is required to ensure the pin reads a defined **HIGH (5 V)** when the button is open. Inside the MCU, the Schmitt trigger input stage treats any voltage **above 0.6 × Vcc = 3.0 V** as logic 1 and **below 1.5 V** as logic 0. Pressing the button firmly drives the pin to 0 V, well within the LOW threshold. --- ### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** *[7 marks]* Interface an **LM35 temperature sensor** to the **ADC0** channel of an ATmega328P. Write a program that reads the temperature every second and turns ON a **red LED** if the temperature exceeds **40 °C**, otherwise a **green LED** remains ON. Show all conversion calculations. **(b)** *[7 marks]* An **LDR (Light Dependent Resistor)** is connected in a voltage divider with a **10 kΩ fixed resistor** to ADC1. The reference voltage is 5 V. Derive the formula for the light intensity (in terms of LDR resistance) and write a program to display "DARK" or "BRIGHT" on the Serial Monitor based on a threshold of **2.5 V**. --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(a) **Conversion Derivation (full steps):** - LM35 output: $V_{out} = 10\ \text{mV/°C} \times T$ - ATmega328P ADC: $V_{out} = \dfrac{ADC}{1023} \times V_{ref} = \dfrac{ADC}{1023} \times 5$ - Equating: $T = \dfrac{ADC \times 5}{1023 \times 0.010} = \dfrac{ADC \times 5}{10.23} \approx ADC \times 0.4883\ ^\circ\text{C}$ **Threshold for 40 °C:** $$ADC_{40°C} = \frac{40 \times 10.23}{5} = 81.84 \approx 82$$ **Complete Sketch:** ```c const int LM35_PIN = A0; const int RED_LED = 8; const int GREEN_LED = 9; const int THRESHOLD = 82; // ~40 °C void setup() { pinMode(RED_LED, OUTPUT); pinMode(GREEN_LED, OUTPUT); Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int adc = analogRead(LM35_PIN); float voltage = adc * (5.0 / 1023.0); float tempC = voltage / 0.010; if (tempC > 40.0) { digitalWrite(RED_LED, LOW); // active-low: ON digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, HIGH); // OFF } else { digitalWrite(RED_LED, HIGH); digitalWrite(GREEN_LED, LOW); } Serial.print("Temp: "); Serial.print(tempC, 1); Serial.println(" °C"); delay(1000); } ``` --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q-B(b) **Voltage Divider Analysis:** - $V_{out} = V_{ref} \times \dfrac{R_{fixed}}{R_{LDR} + R_{fixed}} = 5 \times \dfrac{10{,}000}{R_{LDR} + 10{,}000}$ Solving for $R_{LDR}$: $$R_{LDR} = 10{,}000 \times \left(\dfrac{5}{V_{out}} - 1\right)$$ **At threshold 2.5 V:** $$R_{LDR} = 10{,}000 \times (2 - 1) = 10{,}000\ \Omega = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$$ **Threshold ADC value:** $$ADC_{th} = \frac{2.5}{5} \times 1023 = 511.5 \approx 512$$ **Complete Sketch:** ```c const int LDR_PIN = A1; const int THRESHOLD_ADC = 512; // ~2.5 V void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); } void loop() { int adc = analogRead(LDR_PIN); float voltage = adc * (5.0 / 1023.0); float rLDR = 10000.0 * (5.0 / voltage - 1.0); if (voltage < 2.5) { Serial.println("DARK"); } else { Serial.println("BRIGHT"); } Serial.print("V = "); Serial.print(voltage, 2); Serial.print(" V, R_LDR = "); Serial.print(rLDR, 0); Serial.println(" ohm"); delay(500); } ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Wrong denominator:** Students often write `ADC * 5 / 1024` instead of `/ 1023`. The correct ADC range is **0 to 1023 inclusive**, so always use **1023** in the divisor. (Lose up to 1 mark.) > 2. **Confusing active-high/active-low LED:** Forgetting to invert the logic when using a current-sinking topology. Pressing the button turns the LED OFF if the code is wrong. (Lose up to 1 mark.) > 3. **No debounce:** A 14-mark question that ignores debounce is treated as incomplete. Examiners specifically look for the `millis() - lastDebounceTime > DEBOUNCE_DELAY` pattern. (Lose up to 2 marks.) > 4. **Missing pull-up resistor in schematic:** Drawing a switch between MCU pin and GND *without* a pull-up will lose the design-marks portion. (Lose up to 1 mark.) > 5. **Wrong ADC channel notation:** Using `analogRead(0)` is fine on Arduino, but for the **ATmega328P register-level** question, you must reference `ADMUX` with `MUX0..MUX3` bits set. (Lose up to 1 mark.) > 6. **No decoupling capacitor on LM35 Vcc pin:** A 100 nF ceramic capacitor close to the LM35 is good engineering practice and may earn a grace mark. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - 🔹 **LED interfacing is current-controlled**, not voltage-controlled. Always size $R$ from $(V_{cc} - V_F)/I_F$. - 🔹 **Current-sinking** is preferred for high-current loads; **current-sourcing** is preferred for P-channel high-side switches. - 🔹 **Switches need debouncing** — software delay (≥ 20 ms) or RC + Schmitt trigger hardware filter. - 🔹 **Pull-up resistors** (10 kΩ external or 20–50 kΩ internal) define a stable HIGH level for an open switch. - 🔹 **Active-low convention:** Pressed switch = 0 V (LOW). It is the most common lab convention and is **noise-immune** because pressing cannot generate a glitch as easily as releasing. - 🔹 **ADC formula:** $V = (ADC / 1023) \times V_{ref}$. Use **1023**, not 1024. - 🔹 **LM35 scale factor:** **10 mV / °C** (linear, no offset). - 🔹 **LDR voltage divider:** $R_{LDR} = R_{fixed} \times (V_{ref}/V_{out} - 1)$. - 🔹 **PIR sensor HC-SR501** outputs a digital HIGH (3.3 V) for ~3 s on motion — no ADC needed. - 🔹 **DHT11** uses a proprietary 1-wire protocol — never use `analogRead` on it. - 🔹 **Absolute max current** per ATmega328P pin: **40 mA**; per package: **200 mA**; per port: **100 mA** source, **150 mA** sink. - 🔹 **Total power budget check** is **mandatory** in design questions — examiners will not give full marks without it. - 🔹 **ADC sampling time** must be > 1/(ADC clock) — for full 10-bit accuracy, ADC clock must be **50–200 kHz**. - 🔹 **Bouncing** is **only** a problem for **mechanical** switches — solid-state sensors (Hall, PIR, optical) do **not** bounce. - 🔹 **Internal pull-ups** save board space but have high tolerance (~20–50 kΩ) — use **external 10 kΩ** for noise-sensitive industrial applications. - 🔹 **Watchdog timer (WDT)** is preferred over long `delay()` calls in production firmware, but for KTU lab viva, simple polling with `millis()` is sufficient.

topics.title: Design Systems to Control Physical Actuation Devices like Hydraulic Piston and Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL507 - MICROCONTROLLER LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design Systems to Control Physical Actuation Devices like Hydraulic Piston and Motors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> *An **Actuation System** is an embedded electro-mechanical sub-system in which a low-power microcontroller (e.g., ATmega328P, ARM Cortex-M, 8051) generates control signals (PWM / GPIO / Serial) that are level-shifted and amplified by a power-driver stage to physically move a load via an **actuator** — commonly a DC motor, stepper motor, servo motor, or a hydraulic/solenoid-driven piston.*

A complete design of such a system integrates four engineering layers:

1. **Decision Layer (Microcontroller):** Generates timing/logic signals.
2. **Driver Layer (Power Electronics):** H-bridge, relay, MOSFET/IGBT, or solenoid-driver.
3. **Actuation Layer (Energy Conversion):** Converts electrical energy into mechanical motion.
4. **Feedback Layer (Sensors):** Position encoders, pressure transducers, current sensors, limit switches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Expected Outcome (CO Mapping):**
> Students must be able to *design, simulate (Proteus/Tinkercad), implement, and debug* a closed-loop or open-loop microcontroller-based actuation system, and *justify component selection* for the driver and power stage.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Human-Body Analogy (Easy to Remember for Exams):**
> Think of a robotic arm on a factory floor.
> - The **microcontroller** is the **brain** of the system.
> - The **sensor** (e.g., LVDT, encoder) is the **nerve ending** telling the brain where the hand is.
> - The **driver IC** (L298N, BTS7960) is the **muscle fiber** — it amplifies a tiny brain signal into a strong motion.
> - The **DC motor / hydraulic piston** is the **arm itself** — the thing that actually moves the load.
> - The **PWM signal** is the **rate of nerve firing** — higher duty cycle = more force/speed.

Just as you cannot lift a 20 kg box with your finger (your "logic" alone is too weak), a microcontroller's 5 V / 20 mA GPIO pin cannot drive a 12 V / 2 A DC motor directly. You *need* a power-driver stage — exactly like how your brain needs muscles.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Important Numbers (Memorize):**
> - **Arduino UNO PWM frequency on pin 9, 10, 11: 490 Hz** (Pins 5, 6: 980 Hz).
> - **Standard DC hobby motor rated voltage: 6 V – 12 V DC**, current 100 mA – 2 A.
> - **L298N driver:** Dual H-bridge, **V_s (logic): 5 V**, **V_s (motor): up to 46 V**, **peak current: 2 A per channel**.
> - **Hydraulic pressure (P) in SI = N/m² (Pascal)**. Standard industrial hydraulics: **70 bar – 210 bar** (1 bar ≈ 10⁵ Pa).
> - **Servo motor PWM period = 20 ms (50 Hz)**, pulse width **1 ms = 0°**, **1.5 ms = 90°**, **2 ms = 180°**.
> - **Stepper motor step angle:** 1.8° (200 steps/rev) for NEMA 17.

## 1.4 Two Major Classes of Actuators in KTU Labs

| Class | Example | Control Signal | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrical Rotational** | DC Motor, Servo, Stepper | PWM / Direction logic | Wheels, robotic joints, CNC |
| **Electrical → Fluid → Linear** | Solenoid Valve + Hydraulic Piston | GPIO ON/OFF (or PWM for proportional) | Heavy lifting, press machines, vehicle brakes |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Block Chain

Every actuation system follows this canonical signal-flow chain:

```
Sensor → Microcontroller (ADC / GPIO) → Decision Logic → Driver IC → Actuator → Mechanical Load
   ↑                                                                                        ↓
   └────────────────────── Feedback Loop (Closed-Loop only) ──────────────────────────────┘
```

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Sensing** — A sensor (potentiometer, LVDT, pressure transducer, Hall-effect current sensor) converts a physical quantity into a voltage.
2. **Conditioning** — Signal may need amplification (op-amp), filtering (RC), or level-shifting to match the MCU's **0–5 V ADC range** (or **0–3.3 V** for ESP32/ARM).
3. **Microcontroller Decision** — An ADC reads the analog voltage, software compares it to a setpoint, computes an error, and applies a control law (ON-OFF, P, PI, or PID).
4. **PWM Generation** — The MCU outputs a Pulse-Width Modulated square wave whose **duty cycle D** is proportional to the controller output.
5. **Power Amplification** — The driver IC (L298N, BTS7960, IRF540 MOSFET, or relay) multiplies the small logic signal into a high-current/voltage output.
6. **Actuation** — Motor spins faster/slower, piston extends/retracts faster/slower.
7. **Feedback** — Position/pressure sensor updates the MCU → loop continues.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is this High-Yield?** KTU examiners *love* asking students to draw and label this block diagram. A clean, labeled diagram can secure **3–4 marks** by itself.

## 2.2 PWM — The Heart of Modern Motor Control

A PWM signal has three fundamental parameters:

- **Period (T):** Total time of one cycle.
- **Frequency (f = 1/T):** Switching rate.
- **Duty Cycle (D):** Fraction of the period the signal is HIGH.

$$D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} \times 100\%$$

The **average DC voltage** seen by the motor is:

$$V_{avg} = D \times V_{supply}$$

This is the *fundamental trick* — by varying D from 0% to 100%, you continuously vary the apparent DC voltage from 0 V to V_supply, and hence the motor speed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not vary V_supply with a variable resistor (rheostat)?**
> Because rheostats dissipate the excess power as heat ($$P = I^2 R_{drop}$$), wasting up to 50% energy. PWM switching transistors are either fully ON (low R) or fully OFF (no current) — efficiency exceeds **90%**.

## 2.3 DC Motor Speed Equation (Back-EMF Model)

For a Permanent-Magnet DC (PMDC) motor, the steady-state speed equation is:

$$N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$$

Where:

- $N$ = motor speed in **rad/s** (or RPM after multiplying by 60/2π)
- $V$ = applied armature voltage
- $I_a$ = armature current
- $R_a$ = armature resistance
- $K$ = motor constant
- $\Phi$ = magnetic flux (constant for PMDC)

For PWM control, substitute $V = D \cdot V_{supply}$:

$$N(D) = \frac{D \cdot V_{supply} - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$$

## 2.4 Hydraulic Piston Force Equation

A hydraulic cylinder produces linear force based on Pascal's law:

$$F = P \times A$$

Where:

- $F$ = linear force produced by the piston in **Newtons (N)**
- $P$ = fluid pressure in **Pascals (Pa = N/m²)**
- $A$ = piston cross-sectional area in **m²** = $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ (for a circular piston of diameter $D$)

The **flow rate** determines the **piston velocity**:

$$v = \frac{Q}{A}$$

Where $Q$ = volumetric flow rate in **m³/s**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "*P × A = Force, Q / A = Velocity*" — write this on the first page of your answer sheet.

## 2.5 PID Control (Closed-Loop Master Equation)

The discrete-time PID law used inside the microcontroller is:

$$u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

In microcontroller code, this becomes a *difference equation*:

$$u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i T_s \sum_{i=0}^{k} e[i] + K_d \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{T_s}$$

Where:

- $e[k]$ = error at sample $k$ = (setpoint - measured value)
- $u[k]$ = controller output → fed to PWM duty cycle
- $T_s$ = sampling period (e.g., 10 ms)
- $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = tuning gains

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Beginners often forget the **derivative kick** — when the setpoint changes suddenly, $de/dt$ spikes and causes a huge control spike. Always derivative-filter or compute derivative on **measurement only**, not on error.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Memorize This Table)

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T} \times 100$ | PWM duty cycle | % |
| 2 | $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ | Average motor voltage | V |
| 3 | $N = \frac{V - I_a R_a}{K\Phi}$ | DC motor speed | rad/s |
| 4 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Hydraulic piston force | N |
| 5 | $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ | Piston area | m² |
| 6 | $v = \frac{Q}{A}$ | Piston velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $u[k] = K_p e + K_i \sum e + K_d \Delta e$ | Discrete PID | PWM units |
| 8 | $V_{ADC} = \frac{ADC_{read}}{1023} \times V_{ref}$ | ADC conversion | V |
| 9 | $P_{diss} = I^2 R_{DS(on)}$ | MOSFET heat loss | W |
| 10 | $I_{max,cont} = \frac{T_{j,max} - T_a}{R_{th,JA} \cdot R_{DS(on)}}$ | Safe MOSFET current | A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Notice the use of `\vert` instead of `|` in formulas — this is critical when rendering inside markdown tables to avoid syntax-breaking.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive:** Electronic Stability Control uses a **microcontroller + solenoid-valve-controlled hydraulic brake piston** to pulse individual wheel brakes 15 times/second.
- **Industrial Robotics:** 6-axis robotic arms use **BLDC servo motors** with **PWM + encoder feedback** to achieve ±0.05 mm repeatability.
- **Aerospace:** Flight-control surfaces use **electro-hydraulic actuators (EHA)** where a microcontroller commands a servo valve regulating hydraulic flow.
- **Consumer:** Your 3D printer's extruder is a **NEMA 17 stepper** driven by **A4988 driver** under **microcontroller PID temperature control** of the hot-end.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why PWM Controls DC Motor Speed

We start from the **PMDC motor's back-EMF equation**. When the motor is spinning, it acts as a generator and produces a back-EMF voltage:

$$E_b = K \Phi \omega$$

Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the armature circuit:

$$V_s = I_a R_a + E_b$$

Substituting $E_b$:

$$V_s = I_a R_a + K \Phi \omega$$

Solving for the angular speed $\omega$:

$$\omega = \frac{V_s - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$$

> **Conversion Logic (Step 1):** This is the no-load and loaded speed equation. We see that $\omega$ depends linearly on $V_s$.

Now apply a PWM signal of duty cycle $D$ and amplitude $V_{supply}$ instead of a constant DC voltage. The **average voltage** over one PWM period is:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T} v(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{D \cdot T} V_{supply} \, dt = D \cdot V_{supply}$$

> **Conversion Logic (Step 2):** Because the motor's mechanical time-constant is **much larger** than the PWM period (e.g., 100 ms vs 1 ms), the motor "sees" only the average voltage, not the ripple. This is the **low-pass filtering property of motor inertia**.

Substituting $V_s \rightarrow D \cdot V_{supply}$:

$$\boxed{\omega(D) = \frac{D \cdot V_{supply} - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}}$$

> **Conversion Logic (Step 3):** This is the *controllable speed law*. By varying $D$ from 0 → 1, $\omega$ varies from a small negative value (or zero) to nearly the no-load maximum.

### Worked Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A PMDC motor has $R_a = 2 \, \Omega$, $K\Phi = 0.05 \, \text{V·s/rad}$. Supply is 12 V. At $D = 0.6$ and $I_a = 1 \, \text{A}$, find the speed in RPM.

**Solution:**

$$\omega = \frac{(0.6)(12) - (1)(2)}{0.05} = \frac{7.2 - 2}{0.05} = \frac{5.2}{0.05} = 104 \, \text{rad/s}$$

Convert to RPM:

$$N = 104 \times \frac{60}{2\pi} = 104 \times 9.549 \approx 993 \, \text{RPM}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hydraulic Piston Force vs. Electrical Equivalent

For a hydraulic cylinder with bore (piston) diameter $D$ and rod diameter $d$, the **effective areas** during extension and retraction differ:

**Extension (full bore area):**

$$A_{ext} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$$

**Retraction (annular area):**

$$A_{ret} = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 - d^2)$$

The linear force produced:

$$F = P \times A_{eff}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** During retraction, the same pressure produces *less* force because fluid acts on a smaller annular area. The retraction force is:

$$F_{ret} = P \times \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$

### Numerical Example (Board-Style)

> **Problem:** A hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 50 \, \text{mm}$ and rod $d = 30 \, \text{mm}$. Supply pressure is $P = 100 \, \text{bar}$ ($10^7 \, \text{Pa}$). Calculate extension and retraction forces.

**Solution:**

Extension area:

$$A_{ext} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$F_{ext} = 10^7 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-3} = 19635 \, \text{N} \approx 19.6 \, \text{kN}$$

Retraction area:

$$A_{ret} = \frac{\pi}{4} [(0.05)^2 - (0.03)^2] = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.0025 - 0.0009) = 1.257 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$$

$$F_{ret} = 10^7 \times 1.257 \times 10^{-3} = 12568 \, \text{N} \approx 12.6 \, \text{kN}$$

> **Conversion Logic (Step Final):** Force asymmetry is a fundamental design constraint. When choosing a hydraulic cylinder for a press-lift application, you must size $F_{ext}$ for the lifting load; when used as a pulling actuator (e.g., plough retract), you size for $F_{ret}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PID Output → PWM Duty Cycle Mapping

The microcontroller computes a PID output $u[k]$ which is typically a **floating-point number** (e.g., 153.7). This must be mapped to the integer PWM range of the timer (e.g., 0–255 for Arduino 8-bit PWM).

The mapping is:

$$\text{PWM}_{value} = \text{constrain}\!\left(\text{round}\!\left(\frac{u[k]}{u_{max}} \times 255\right), 0, 255\right)$$

Where $u_{max}$ is the maximum expected controller output (e.g., the setpoint × plant gain).

> **Conversion Logic:** If the plant reaches setpoint with $u[k] = u_{max}$ at full demand, then $u_{max}$ defines the *normalization ceiling*. A common practical choice is to set $u_{max}$ equal to the *setpoint value* in the same engineering unit.

### Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A position-control PID loop has setpoint = 1000 (encoder counts). $K_p = 2$, error $e = 50$ counts. $u_{max} = 1000$. Compute the PWM output.

**Solution:**

Proportional component:

$$u_P = K_p \cdot e = 2 \times 50 = 100$$

PWM value:

$$\text{PWM} = \frac{100}{1000} \times 255 = 25.5 \approx 26$$

Duty cycle:

$$D = \frac{26}{255} \times 100\% \approx 10.2\%$$

> **Conversion Logic (Final):** A small error (5% of setpoint) yields a small PWM (10%) — a sensible, well-tuned response. If $D$ saturates at 100% for tiny errors, the system is *over-gained* and will oscillate.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop)

| Parameter | Open-Loop PWM Control | Closed-Loop PID Control |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Poor — depends on load, battery voltage | Excellent — self-corrects |
| Cost | Low (no sensor) | Higher (sensor + ADC + code) |
| Complexity | Trivial | Moderate (tuning required) |
| Robustness to disturbance | None | High |
| KTU Exam Expectation | Asked in **3-mark** questions | Asked in **14-mark** design questions |
| Example | Toy DC fan speed knob | CNC spindle RPM regulator |

> [!TIP]
> If a KTU question says *"design a system that maintains constant motor speed even when load varies"* — **always choose closed-loop PID**, never open-loop. Examiners specifically test whether you know *why* feedback is needed.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram: Generic Actuation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Sensors: Potentiometer, Encoder, Pressure Transducer] --> node2[Signal Conditioning: Op-Amp, RC Filter, Level Shifter]
    node2 --> node3[Microcontroller: ATmega328P / ARM / 8051]
    node3 --> node4[Software Layer: ADC, PID Algorithm, PWM Generator]
    node4 --> node5[Driver IC: L298N, BTS7960, MOSFET H-Bridge, Relay]
    node5 --> node6[Actuator: DC Motor, Servo, Stepper, Solenoid Valve]
    node6 --> node7[Mechanical Load: Wheel, Robotic Joint, Hydraulic Piston, Conveyor]
    node1 -. Feedback Path .-> node3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows are the **forward path** (command flow). The **dotted feedback arrow** from sensors back to the MCU closes the loop — this is what makes it a *closed-loop control system*.

---

## 4.2 H-Bridge Direction Control Schematic (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[V_s Battery 12V] --> nodeQ1[Q1: P-MOSFET Top Left]
    nodeA --> nodeQ2[Q2: P-MOSFET Top Right]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeM[Motor Terminal A]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeM2[Motor Terminal B]
    nodeM --> nodeQ3[Q3: N-MOSFET Bottom Left]
    nodeM2 --> nodeQ4[Q4: N-MOSFET Bottom Right]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeGND[Ground 0V]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeGND
    nodeMCU[Microcontroller GPIO Pins] --> nodeGate1[Gate Driver 1]
    nodeMCU --> nodeGate2[Gate Driver 2]
    nodeGate1 --> nodeQ1
    nodeGate1 --> nodeQ3
    nodeGate2 --> nodeQ2
    nodeGate2 --> nodeQ4
```

> **Operating Modes (KTU Favorite):**
> - **Q1 + Q4 ON, Q2 + Q3 OFF** → Current flows A → B → motor spins **Forward**.
> - **Q2 + Q3 ON, Q1 + Q4 OFF** → Current flows B → A → motor spins **Reverse**.
> - **Q1 + Q2 ON, Q3 + Q4 OFF** → Both motor terminals pulled to V_s → **Brake / Regen**.
> - **All OFF** → Motor coasts to stop (high-impedance).
> - **Q1 + Q3 ON simultaneously** → **SHOOT-THROUGH** → short circuit → MOSFETs explode. *Always include dead-time in code.*

---

## 4.3 Hydraulic Piston System with Microcontroller Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePWR[Hydraulic Pump + Reservoir] --> nodeV[Pressure Relief Valve]
    nodeV --> nodeDCV[4-3 Directional Control Valve / Solenoid]
    nodeDCV --> nodeCYL[Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder]
    nodeCYL --> nodeLOAD[Mechanical Load Press Arm]
    nodePOS[LVDT Position Sensor] --> nodeADC[Microcontroller ADC Pin]
    nodeADC --> nodeMCU[Microcontroller: Reads Position, Computes Error, Drives Solenoid]
    nodeMCU --> nodeDRV[Relay / Solenoid Driver ULN2003]
    nodeDRV --> nodeDCV
    nodePOS -. Feedback .-> nodeADC
```

> **Operating Principle:** The microcontroller energizes one solenoid of the 4/3 valve to extend the piston, the other to retract, and de-energizes both to hold position. By **PWM-ing** a *proportional solenoid*, you can achieve infinitely variable piston velocity.

---

## 4.4 Closed-Loop PID Control Flow (Software + Hardware Combined)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeSP[User Setpoint: Desired RPM or Position] --> nodeERR[Compute Error: e = Setpoint - Measured]
    nodeERR --> nodePID[PID Computation: Kp.e + Ki.Integral e + Kd.Delta e]
    nodePID --> nodeMAP[Map to PWM Range: 0 to 255]
    nodeMAP --> nodePWM[Write PWM to Timer Register OCRx]
    nodePWM --> nodeDRV[Driver IC: H-Bridge or Solenoid Driver]
    nodeDRV --> nodeMOTOR[DC Motor or Hydraulic Actuator]
    nodeMOTOR --> nodeENC[Encoder / LVDT / Pressure Sensor]
    nodeENC --> nodeREAD[Read via Interrupt or Polling]
    nodeREAD --> nodeERR
```

> **Insight for Exam:** This is a **classic block diagram of a sampled-data control system**. The sample period $T_s$ is set by the timer ISR (Interrupt Service Routine) — typically 5–20 ms for motor control.

---

## 4.5 Component-Level Architecture (Practical Lab Wiring)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodePSU[External 12V / 24V SMPS] --> nodeDRV[L298N Motor Driver V_s Terminal]
    nodeARD[Arduino UNO 5V Logic] --> nodeDRV5[L298N 5V Logic Pin]
    nodeARD --> nodePWM1[PWM Pin 9 to L298N ENA]
    nodeARD --> nodePWM2[PWM Pin 10 to L298N ENB]
    nodeARD --> nodeDIR1[Digital Pin 2 to IN1]
    nodeARD --> nodeDIR2[Digital Pin 3 to IN2]
    nodeARD --> nodeDIR3[Digital Pin 4 to IN3]
    nodeARD --> nodeDIR4[Digital Pin 5 to IN4]
    nodeDRV --> nodeM1[DC Motor Channel A]
    nodeDRV --> nodeM2[DC Motor Channel B]
    nodeARD --> nodeSER[Servo Signal Pin to Servo]
    nodeARD --> nodeADC[Potentiometer Wiper to A0]
    nodePOTSUP[5V Supply] --> nodePOT[10k Potentiometer]
    nodePOT --> nodeADC
```

> **Practical Note:** L298N's logic supply (5V) and motor supply (12V) are *separate* — never tie them to the same rail if motor current is high. Add **flyback diodes** (1N4007) across motor terminals to protect against inductive kickback.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: Define duty cycle of a PWM signal. Why is PWM preferred over a variable resistor for DC motor speed control?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **duty cycle (D)** of a PWM signal is the ratio of the ON-time to the total period, expressed as a percentage:

$$D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$$

PWM is preferred over a rheostat (variable resistor) for motor speed control for two reasons:

1. **High efficiency:** In PWM, the switching transistor is either fully ON (low $V_{DS}$, low dissipation) or fully OFF (zero current, zero dissipation). Power loss $P = I^2 R$ is minimal. In a rheostat, the "extra" voltage is dropped across the resistor as heat, giving efficiencies as low as **30–50%**.
2. **Better speed regulation:** The average voltage $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$ is independent of load current over a wide range, giving a more stable speed under varying torque.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Q: What is the function of an H-bridge driver IC? Name two commonly used H-bridge ICs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **H-bridge** is a power-electronic circuit consisting of four switches (transistors / MOSFETs) arranged in an "H" topology around a DC motor. It allows the motor to be driven in **both directions (forward and reverse)** and also to **brake / regen** by reconfiguring which switches are ON.

It is needed because a microcontroller GPIO can only source or sink current in *one* direction, but a motor needs bidirectional current for bidirectional rotation.

Two commonly used H-bridge ICs:
1. **L298N** — dual H-bridge, up to 46 V, 2 A continuous.
2. **BTS7960** — high-current (43 A) half-bridge, often used in pairs for a full H-bridge.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Design a microcontroller-based closed-loop system to maintain the speed of a 12 V DC motor at 1500 RPM irrespective of load variations. Specify the hardware components, draw the block diagram, and write the PID algorithm in pseudo-code.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Hardware Design & Block Diagram

**Model Solution:**

**Components Required:**

| # | Component | Specification | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Microcontroller | ATmega328P (Arduino UNO) | PID computation, PWM generation |
| 2 | DC Motor | 12 V PMDC, 2000 RPM no-load | Actuator |
| 3 | Motor Driver | L298N dual H-bridge | Current & voltage amplification |
| 4 | Speed Sensor | Optical encoder, 360 pulses/rev | Feedback (closed-loop) |
| 5 | Power Supply | 12 V / 3 A SMPS | Motor power |
| 6 | Logic Supply | 5 V regulator (LM7805) | MCU & logic |
| 7 | Flyback Diodes | 1N4007 × 4 | Protection |

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes)

**Pin Assignment:**

- PWM output → Arduino pin 9 → L298N ENA (enable A)
- Direction control → pins 2, 3 → L298N IN1, IN2
- Encoder channel A → Arduino pin 2 (external interrupt INT0)
- Encoder channel B → Arduino pin 3

**Design Justification (1 mark):** The encoder produces 360 pulses per revolution. At 1500 RPM = 25 rev/s, the pulse frequency is $25 \times 360 = 9000 \, \text{Hz}$, well within ATmega328P's interrupt-handling capability.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: PID Algorithm in Pseudocode

**Model Solution:**

```c
// Pseudo-code — Arduino C for clarity (lab course context)
#define PPR 360          // Encoder pulses per revolution
#define SETPOINT_RPM 1500
#define TS 0.01          // Sample time = 10 ms

// PID gains (tuned for the specific motor)
float Kp = 1.2, Ki = 0.5, Kd = 0.05;

float integral = 0.0;
float prevError = 0.0;
volatile unsigned long pulseCount = 0;   // updated in ISR

// Interrupt: count encoder pulses
void encoderISR() {
    pulseCount++;
}

void setup() {
    pinMode(9, OUTPUT);          // PWM pin
    pinMode(2, INPUT_PULLUP);    // Encoder A
    attachInterrupt(0, encoderISR, RISING);
}

void loop() {
    static unsigned long lastTime = 0;
    if (millis() - lastTime < 10) return;   // 10 ms sampling
    lastTime = millis();

    // 1) Measure speed
    unsigned long pulses = pulseCount;
    pulseCount = 0;
    float rpm = (pulses / (float)PPR) * (60000.0 / 10.0);

    // 2) Compute error
    float error = SETPOINT_RPM - rpm;

    // 3) PID computation
    integral += error * TS;
    float derivative = (error - prevError) / TS;
    float output = Kp * error + Ki * integral + Kd * derivative;
    prevError = error;

    // 4) Map to PWM 0–255
    int pwm = constrain((int)(output * 255.0 / 1000.0), 0, 255);
    analogWrite(9, pwm);
}
```

**Explanation of the algorithm (3 marks):**

1. The encoder ISR counts pulses asynchronously.
2. Every 10 ms, the main loop reads the pulse count, computes the RPM using the formula $\text{RPM} = \frac{\text{pulses}}{PPR} \times \frac{60000}{T_{sample\_ms}}$.
3. The error is the difference between setpoint and measured RPM.
4. The PID law computes a control output; integral term accumulates past error to eliminate steady-state offset; derivative term predicts future error to dampen overshoot.
5. The output is mapped to 0–255 PWM range and written to the motor via L298N.
6. The cycle repeats — forming a **closed-loop feedback system** that automatically compensates for load changes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students forget to **reset `pulseCount`** at the start of each sample — leading to a constantly increasing RPM reading and a violently oscillating motor. Also, declaring `pulseCount` as `volatile` is *mandatory* because it is modified inside an ISR and read in the main loop. Lose **2 marks** if you miss this.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a microcontroller-based hydraulic piston control system. Discuss the role of proportional solenoid valves and pressure feedback.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Block Diagram & Working

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes)

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

1. The **hydraulic pump** draws oil from the reservoir and supplies high-pressure fluid (typically 100–200 bar) to a **4/3-way proportional directional control valve (DCV)**.
2. The **microcontroller** generates a PWM signal whose duty cycle determines the **spool position** of the DCV — which in turn controls the **flow rate and direction** of oil to the cylinder.
3. The **double-acting hydraulic cylinder** converts oil pressure into linear motion of the piston rod. The piston extends or retracts based on which side of the cylinder is pressurized.
4. A **position sensor (LVDT or rotary encoder)** on the piston rod measures the actual position and feeds it back to the microcontroller's ADC.
5. The microcontroller implements a **closed-loop PID algorithm** to make the piston track a desired trajectory (e.g., extend 100 mm in 2 seconds, hold, retract).
6. A **pressure transducer** monitors line pressure; if pressure exceeds a safety limit (e.g., due to a stuck load), the microcontroller **cuts off the solenoid** and triggers an alarm.

**Key Equations (2 marks):**

- Piston force: $F = P \times A$
- Piston velocity: $v = Q / A$
- Spool flow (proportional valve): $Q = K_v \cdot D \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$ (approximate, where $K_v$ is valve flow coefficient and $D$ is spool opening proportional to PWM duty cycle).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Proportional Solenoid Valves & Pressure Feedback

**Model Solution:**

**Proportional Solenoid Valve (3 marks):**

Unlike an ON/OFF solenoid (which either fully opens or fully closes), a **proportional solenoid** has a continuously variable spool position controlled by the current through its coil. The microcontroller drives the coil via a **PWM signal** (typically 100–200 Hz) and a **current regulator** (often an op-amp + MOSFET + shunt resistor in a closed-loop current control).

- Higher duty cycle → higher average current → stronger magnetic force → spool moves further → more oil flow.
- The relationship between coil current and spool position is **approximately linear** over the operating range, allowing precise flow control.

**Pressure Feedback (2 marks):**

A **strain-gauge pressure transducer** mounted on the cylinder port produces a **4–20 mA current loop** (or 0–10 V) proportional to oil pressure. The signal is fed to the MCU's ADC. The microcontroller can:

- Limit maximum force by capping pressure (over-pressure protection).
- Implement a **force-control loop** by closing the loop on pressure rather than position.
- Detect end-of-stroke (pressure spike) and shut off the pump to save energy.

**Real-World Example (2 marks):**

In **injection-molding machines**, the mold-clamp unit uses a hydraulic piston controlled by a microcontroller-driven proportional valve with **position + pressure dual feedback**. The clamp force is maintained at, say, 1000 kN with ±2% accuracy throughout the molding cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often draw the hydraulic cylinder but forget the **pressure-relief valve** in the schematic. This is a **safety-critical** component and examiners award **1 mark** specifically for including it. Lose this mark if you omit it.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam 5-Minute Revision List:**

- [ ] **PWM definition:** $D = T_{ON} / T$; $V_{avg} = D \cdot V_s$.
- [ ] **DC motor speed:** $\omega = (V - I_a R_a) / (K\Phi)$.
- [ ] **Hydraulic force:** $F = P \times A = P \times \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$.
- [ ] **Hydraulic velocity:** $v = Q / A$.
- [ ] **Piston retraction area is smaller** than extension area (annular, not full bore).
- [ ] **H-bridge has 4 modes:** Forward, Reverse, Brake, Coast. Never turn on both high-side and low-side on the same leg → shoot-through.
- [ ] **L298N limits:** 46 V, 2 A continuous per channel.
- [ ] **Arduino PWM freq:** Pins 9/10/11 = 490 Hz; pins 5/6 = 980 Hz.
- [ ] **Servo PWM period** = 20 ms; pulse 1 ms = 0°, 1.5 ms = 90°, 2 ms = 180°.
- [ ] **PID discrete form:** $u[k] = K_p e + K_i T_s \sum e + K_d (e[k] - e[k-1]) / T_s$.
- [ ] **Always use `volatile`** for any variable shared between an ISR and main loop.
- [ ] **Always include flyback diodes** across inductive loads (motors, solenoids, relays).
- [ ] **Open-loop vs closed-loop:** Closed-loop is required when the question says "*maintain*", "*regulate*", or "*track*".
- [ ] **Block diagram order (memorize):** Sensor → MCU → Driver → Actuator → Load → Sensor (loop closed).
- [ ] **Standard lab encoder:** 360 pulses/rev (or 1×, 2×, 4× decoding for 360, 720, 1440 counts/rev).
- [ ] **Hydraulic pressure units:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa = 100 kPa.
- [ ] **Safety components:** Pressure relief valve, E-stop relay, current-limiting fuse, optocoupler isolation between logic and power.
- [ ] **Tuning rule of thumb:** Start with $K_i = K_d = 0$, increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillation, then halve it. Add $K_i$ to remove offset. Add small $K_d$ only if needed.
- [ ] **KTU diagram-marker rule:** Always *label every block* and *show direction of signal flow with arrows*. A directionless diagram loses marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When the question says "design a system", always answer in this order:
> 1. State the **objective** and **constraints** (1 mark).
> 2. Draw a **labeled block diagram** (2–3 marks).
> 3. List the **hardware components with specifications** (2–3 marks).
> 4. Explain the **working principle** step by step (3–4 marks).
> 5. Provide the **control algorithm / pseudocode / equations** (3–4 marks).
> 6. Discuss **safety and limitations** (1 mark).
>
> Following this 6-step template guarantees you will not miss a marking head and will score full (or near-full) marks.
